OK, I see the problem. There are two solutions:

1) If you name your checkM file `Chdb.csv`, and put it in the workDirectory folder: `data_tables`, dRep will recognize what it is and load it. You could then just run the command `dRep choose <workDirectory>`, and will pick the best genome from each cluster and population the folder `derplicated_genomes`. You could then run `dRep analyze <workDirectory> -pl a` to make the plots in the `figures` folder. These steps will both run very quickly, so you won't need to re-run any of the previous filtering or clustering steps.

It depends a lot on the testrunner you are using. If you're using `testrepository` and `subunit` as used in the gabbi tests themselves you can provide a pattern to tox that eventually gets passed on down to filter the tests that are run. For example if you clone the gabbi repo and do:

```
$ tox -epy27 -- self
```

that will run just the tests in the `self.yaml` file. You can also choose a specific test

```
tox -epy27 -- self_bogus_method
```

That will run the 'bogus method' test in the self file.

The reason this works is because testr (the short name for testrepository) does discovery to list all the tests and then starts another process to actually run those tests, filtering according to the rules you provide.

`unittest` and similar tools have ways to limit tests when doing discovery but it does it at the level of file name. So, for example, you can:

```
python -m unittest discover -p 'test_intercept*'
```

to get the tests from just the test_intercept file.

So one way to get what you want is to use testr. Another, if you don't like testr, is to have more test_ files, each of which loads a subset of the YAML files and then use the -p thing.

Does any of that get you closer?

If not there might be some other ways worth exploring, but it would be a while before it was released because I want to get 1.0 out the door soon without too many more changes (except for bug fixes).

Pipe.go() should launch the helper and should
return immediatelly. We won't know the tempo
of data creation of the source. If there is something
we dont like we can cancel it. Running on streams make
it usable on variety of situations. Lets think it independent
of Popen. I'm currently using it similar to below example.

```python
class Pipe:
    def __init__(instream, outstream):
        self.in = instream
        self.out = outstream
        self.task = None

    async def go():
        self.task = spawn(dumpstream(self.in, self.out))

pipe= Pipe(instream=zcat.stdout, outstream=groff.stdin)
await pipe.go()

while True:
    rd = await groff.stdout.read()  # Final output we are after. 
    if not rd:
        break

pipe.task.cancel() # Normally not needed, as task will exit when instream is exhausted..
```

Hmm. Currently I'm using levels 1 and 2 in the relations table. The relations table where level = 1 is what defines the DAG (direction encoded by which is "parent" and which is "child"). The entries where level = 2 are essentially a shortcut to avoid traversing the DAG when doing the domain-specific task of getting exons of a gene.  But those 1s and 2s are nominal rather than ordinal values -- things would work the same if I had called them "a" and "b". So you might be able to get away with adding a level 3 to indicate splicing events if it was useful to sort of step outside the hierarchy.

Or another angle: your graph shows CDSs as children of exons but in most GFF/GTF files I've seen, CDSs are siblings of exons and both are children of transcripts. So there is already support for e.g. multiple level-1 children. I think the last 2 rows of this table are all you have to add:

parent    |child      |level
----------|-----------|------
gene      |transcript |1
transcript|exon       |1
transcript|cds        |1
transcript|splice     |1
splice    |exon       |1

Hey @nyobna,

Thanks for passing along the library!

The WeighMeanVar algo uses ffn's calc_mean_var_weights function under the hood. It might not be clear from the docstring, but the bounds parameter is used to restrict the maximum/minimum weights a single security can have. However, as it currently stands, the optimizer always finds a set of weights that adds up to 1. So basically you could pass in bounds (0.05, 0.25) if you wanted to make sure that a single security had at least 5% but no more than 25% allocation. 

Therefore, the WeighMeanVar algo will always return weights that add up to 1. It may be a good idea to add a parameter so that this can changed, but I still have to think about it. Not sure how the optimizer would react to different target sums.

In the meantime, you could do something like this:

```python
class ApplyLeverage(bt.Algo):
    
    def __init__(self, leverage):
        super(ApplyLeverage, self).__init__()
        self.leverage = leverage
    
    def __call__(self, target):
        target.temp['weights'] = target.temp['weights'] * self.leverage
        return True    

s = bt.Strategy('s1', [bt.algos.RunMonthly(),       
                       bt.algos.SelectAll(),                       
                       bt.algos.WeighMeanVar(),                       
                       ApplyLeverage(2.),
                       bt.algos.Rebalance()])

test = bt.Backtest(s, data)
res = bt.run(test)

res.plot()
```

Basically, you are creating a new custom ```Algo``` that will scale up the target weights by a factor of 2. I don't know if this is exactly what you want but the general idea applies. You can create your own algos and modify the target weights according to your needs. 

Hope this helps,
Phil

The TG-51 addendum says it's valid for PDDs between 63% to 86% (Table I). The paper that those values are based on, Muir & Rogers 2010, state that the same values are valid for PDDs between 62.7% and 86.1% (Table III) and is what pylinac uses. Perhaps the TG addendum was being conservative by rounding, and I suppose that's what should happen here. In practice, these limits are rarely hit. I will update the docs and code to follow the addendum values, which seem to be inclusive ("...are valid for 63 <%dd(10)X < 86..."). Thanks for the detailed catch!

Looked through the commits... I like the more library-centric configuration, but that does come with two tradeoffs:
 Not setting up a handler to output to means that it is up to the users of your library to learn logging and how to roll their own handler -not very friendly-, but that does mean it is more flexible for those who wish to figure out what to do. 
Adding in the additional information to each log entry (i.e. your fourth commit) makes debugging much easier, but it kills the "CLI session-log" look that you would get from CLI tools like putty/securecrt/etc.

I like this setup for creating a library debugging log (and might be a very desirable feature just for that), but for the specific use-case of a more traditional "session log" I'm not sure I would use this. Thoughts?

It is returning a None, not a True -- indicating failure.  A status code of 19 is being returned: CIP Status: 0x13 (Not enough data)

You are not specify the data type in your request (so it defaults to SINT):

`... "@4/106/3"=44 ...` 

So, I would guess that, since you are using the CIP Set Attribute Single request, and since you must supply exactly the correct number of SINT (8-bit) data to fully specify the entire CIP Attribute, you probably aren't using the right data type for the Attribute, or it is an Array, meaning that you must supply a number of values greater than 1.

If its a single DINT, try:

`... "@4/106/3=(DINT)44" ...`

It its an array, supply data for all elements (say, for a 5-element DINT array):

`... "@4/106/3=(DINT)44,55,66,77,88" ...`

If it is indeed a DINT array, find out the size of the array, and supply that number of elements.

.mca files are not nbt files. The [Region File Format](https://minecraft.gamepedia.com/Region_file_format) uses several distinct regions of binary data to store minecraft chunks and some of these regions contain compressed nbt data. You can't use `nbtlib` to read .mca files directly. I'm planning to write a python 3 alternative to [`pymclevel`](https://github.com/mcedit/pymclevel) based on `nbtlib` but for now you'll need to extract the nbt regions from the .mca files yourself and feed them to `nbtlib` manually.

Sorry for the delay looking into this issue, I was without a Linux machine for a while.

I think the problem is that your system doesn't have the uinput module. `keyboard` tries to open the file /dev/uinput expecting to create a new device, but ends up just creating a regular file at that location. This can be seen by the mode of the file, `-rw-r--r-- `, instead of `crw-------`, where `c` means "character special file".

uinput is used to ensure we can always send any keys without depending on physical keyboard limitations, or even the presence of a physical keyboard. If your system has a plugged in keyboard we can try removing the dependency on uinput; if it doesn't have either uinput or a plugged in keyboard, I'm out of ideas.

Additionally, I'll add a check to ensure uinput exists before trying to create the file.

This was originally discussed in #49 , and I would consider it to be an error in the salami annotations / the salami parser.  (BTW, which version of the annotations are you using?  And can you point to a specific annotation?)

To summarize, there are a few things happening here:

1. Converting from events to intervals is only possible when intervals are disjoint; otherwise, end-boundaries have to be specified somehow.
2. I don't think the salmai parser should have put that "silence" in, and it may be a mistake.  (Or maybe the Z, depending on whether you're looking at large or function.)
3. We have SALAMI ported to JAMS format [here](https://github.com/marl/jams-data/tree/master/datasets/SALAMI), and using these instead of labs may save you a bunch of headaches.

> In separation, are you using transparency?

Yes.  For each source, we generate a colormap that interpolates from (1,1,1,0) (white, transparent) to `(r,g,b,1)` where `(r,g,b)` is a color sourced from the axis property cycler.  The source is then normalized by the cumulative spectrogram peak (ie aggregating all sources and taking the max over time/frequency), and they are overlayed on top of each other.

We could alternately make it cycle up to `(r,g,b,0.75)` (or something) so that overlapping energy between sources adds up to a new color, but it might be confusing on a white background.  Maybe a better option is to do it starting from a black background?

@JoshuaC3 really appreciate the benchmarking! I've been meaning to do that for some time but well, there's only 24hrs in a day.

I'm surprised mlens fails to outperform mlxtend. mlxtend doesn't actually do stacking (despite the name) - it merely fits the base learners on all data, and then the meta learner on those predictions. Hence the meta learner is trained on base learner training errors, but at test time faces test errors. 

The two reasons for your results that I can think of is (a) if you have very little data the folds will be too noisy or (b) if the data is not i.i.d. the folds will be biased. I hit upon (b) when doing the MNIST benchmark, since creating folds without shuffling the data won't cover all classes. 

As a code integrity check, I spun up a simple mlxtend-vs-mlens benchmark with your models (but the default objective function) on the ``sklearn.datasets.make_friedman1`` synthetic dataset. The SuperLearner does indeed outperform mlxtend (on a hold-out validation set): 

```
MAE:

No. obs |   mlens |  mlxtend |
   5000 |    0.38 |     0.48 |
  10000 |    0.31 |     0.38 |
  15000 |    0.28 |     0.33 |
  20000 |    0.26 |     0.28 |
  25000 |    0.24 |     0.26 |
```
As a sanity check, if you use the ``SequentialEnsemble`` with ``'full'`` as the layer class (e.g. ``add('full', [xgbr, lgbr])``), you should get the *exact* same results as mlxtend. I get: 
```
No. obs |   mlens |  mlxtend |
   5000 |    0.48 |     0.48 |
  10000 |    0.38 |     0.38 |
  15000 |    0.33 |     0.33 |
  20000 |    0.28 |     0.28 |
  25000 |    0.26 |     0.26 |
```
So to me it looks like it should. For your benchmark, would you mind trying  

1. use ``SequentialEnsemble`` with ``'full'`` layers to see if you get the same scores
2. set ``shuffle=True`` (try both in the constructor and in the ``add`` method)
3. use more data (if your dataset < 1000 samples)

Hi @lurajon!

If the first item in the "AR4PI297" column is a pure number (e.g. "1234567"), then the parser will assume that the entire column only contains numbers. Make sure to set the parser to "text".

```html
<th class="sorter-text">...</th>
```

If you mean that "AR30419A" is after "AR4PP175", it's because the natural sort breaks the value into chunks, "AR-30419-A" and "AR-4-PP-175". In this case, 4 is greater than 30419. You might therefore want to use a plain (not natural) text sort. To do this, use the [`textSorter` option](https://mottie.github.io/tablesorter/docs/#textsorter) as follows ([demo](https://jsfiddle.net/Mottie/bbxxomhx/155/)):

Header cell HTML

```html
<th class="plainsort">AlphaNumeric</th>
```

```js
$(function() {
  $('table').tablesorter({
    theme: 'blue',
    widgets: ['zebra'],
    textSorter: {
      '.plainsort': $.tablesorter.sortText
    }
  });
});
```

Also, from looking at the code provided:

* Are you using the pager addon or widget? I see code for both, but only one is needed. I would recommend using the widget but really there isn't a difference.
* I see there are a lot of disabled columns in the `headers: { 0: { sorter: false},... 11: {sorter: false} }` option. An easier way to set this up is to add a "sorter-false" class name to the header

    ```html
    <th class="sorter-text">...</th>
    ```

  or, add any other class name to each header, something like "disabled", then use the `headers` option as follows:

    ```js
    headers: { ".disabled": { sorter: false } }
    ```

So far, I've only updated the `widget-stickyHeaders.js` and dist folder `widget-stickyHeaders.min.js` files. The `jquery.tablesorter.widgets.js` file usually only gets updated when a new release is available.

However, if you are working with a whole library, you should really consider setting up a global configuration file that has all the code directories in it so that `fortpy` can find them all. See [global configuration](https://github.com/rosenbrockc/fortpy/wiki/Fortpy-Global-Configuration) for details. You basically just need a

```xml
<codes>
    <trunk name="uncle" value="~/codes/uncle/src" />
    <trunk name="celib" value="~/codes/symlib/src" />
    <trunk name="enumlib" value="~/codes/enumlib/src" />
  </codes>
```

in the global configuration file with all the paths to your various folders. Then just add `export FORTPY_CONFIG="~/path/to/config.xml"` to your bash profile.

Hi everyone (@josefmonje @teodorgarzdin @bazookon @jayhuang75 )! 

Thank you for waiting patiently and coming up with some alternative solutions (thanks @josefmonje) until I could find the time to look into the issue.

## Service Account
So the first thing I noticed (that is sort of seperate from this issue but also causes permission denied errors) is that when generating a service account we have to make sure the role is set to "Owner":

![Owner](http://i.imgur.com/WtK4GCe.jpg)

## Pyrebase
The second thing is the code in Pyrebase. Reading the [oauth2 docs for service accounts](http://oauth2client.readthedocs.io/en/latest/source/oauth2client.service_account.html) we find: 

>This credential does not require a flow to instantiate because it represents a two legged flow, and therefore has all of the required information to generate and refresh its own access tokens.

So really we should be able to refresh tokens automatically and not run into any permission errors.

The error in the Pyrebase code is that when we were intialize Pyrebase the access_token was cached (instead of generating it dynamically for each api call) which meant we were eventually using stale access tokens for our api calls.

The offending code:
```
self.access_token = self.credentials.get_access_token()
```
So rather than caching we directly store the credentials and call ```get_access_token()``` before each api call.

This has been added with e0f7c59fd48a37348136c0dd7b77a9219c22d22b and is now in the newest release of Pyrebase. I've only tested this change with about ~2 hours of operations so if you could all try it out and let me know how it goes that would be great.

Thanks!

So my thought is

`py3status` - spawns new process (py3) write to i3bar and gets i3bar event messages

`py3` - reads config, starts modules including i3status ones, creates i3bar ready output.

We then use shared memory to communicate.

__py3__ 

writes
- `output` - unicode output in i3bar format
- `updated` - boolean set to true when output has been updated

reads
- `event_queue` - array of event strings

__py3status__

reads
- `update` - checks to see if new output
- `output` - passes straight to i3bar then resets `update`

writes
- `event_queue` appends raw event strings from 3bar

That feels like the most minimal exchange we can get away with, both processes would be checking each other 10 times a second and we'd have some lock contention but it should be fairly minimal.  
we might need to do a little bit around passing start up parameters to `py3` etc but that should be simple.

I was thinking about this some more.

First I thought we should change `time_in(seconds)` so that if we don't supply a time then we use the value in the modules `cache_timeout` so we can just use `self.py3.time_in()` instead of `self.py3.time_in(self.cache_timeout)`.  This would change the behavior slightly when we call `time_in()` (currently this requests an immediate update), but as we have few users at present, now would be the ideal time to do it.

Then I thought why even be this explicit?  If a module methods response does not include `cached_until` then we could just assume that we should use the modules `cache_timeout`, currently we use the value from `py3status --timeout x` so it would be a small change and unlikely to break anything.  we would still use that as the default if a module has no `cache_timeout`.  This has the side effect of allowing the user to set `cache_timeout` for modules that do not currently have it as a parameter.

I think that we should adopt both these ideas.  Also, if we are going to switch modules to use `safe_format()` that it would be a good opportunity to make both changes at the same time (though maybe as separate PRs).

I also think we should sneak this into 3.1

The projects are actually quite similar in features and possibilities so it's mostly a matter of taste :)

Personally I think this project uses a more Pythonic API which makes usage easier and clearer. To illustrate:

This project (as taken from the docs):
```
import statsd
counter = statsd.Counter('MyApplication')
# The actual increment
counter += 1
```
The other project (as taken from the docs):
```
from statsd import StatsClient
statsd = StatsClient()
statsd.incr('MyApplication')
```

I would argue that being able to increase/decrease by using `+=` and `-=` is more obvious but you have the choice available. If you wish, `counter.increment()` is also available.

In the end it mostly comes down to preference :)
If you are using Django you can also take a look at another project of mine which uses this one internally: https://github.com/WoLpH/django-statsd

The aforementioned commit is a *last minute* check to catch datas which may have gotten a filter added which *replays* but doesn't do it through the standard call `cerebro.replaydata`.

An example of such filter is `DayStepsFilter` (which replays a bar in 2 parts: `OHL` + `C`)

If `_doreplay` is set to `True` this happens:

```python
        if self._doreplay:
            # preloading is not supported with replay. full timeframe bars
            # are constructed in realtime
            self._dopreload = False
```

Which simply disables preloading.

Additionally

```python
                strat.qbuffer(self._exactbars, replaying=self._doreplay)
```

which ends up adding an extra position to the internal buffers to allow replaying (and only has effect when `exactbars != 0`)

A quick inspection of the code pointed to you in the links shows no traces of `replaying` being set to `True` in the data feed, i.e.: the pointed commit shouldn't have any effect in your code.

In any case and without knowing how the filter is *breaking* it is rather difficult to understand what could be causing the trouble

> In order to preserve the current behaviour these should default to True.

If the signature is `def floats(min_value=None, max_value=None, allow_inf=True, allow_nan=True)`, then I think a call like `floats(0, 1000)` becomes self contradictory - `allow_inf==True`, but +/-inf is outside the range specified. This is most likely because the `gi` module is missing.

On _Linux_, menus are supported only when using the _GTK_ or _AppIndicator_ backends, both of which are interfaced to through `gi`. Use [HAS_MENU](http://pystray.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference.html#pystray.Icon.HAS_MENU) the check whether the current backend has menu support.

The most common cause for `gi` missing is that you run _pystray_ from a _virtualenv_ that does not have access to system packages. This package, which is part of _GTK_, is deeply connected to the host system and cannot be installed from _pypi_.

Instead of creating the `app` and `ask` variables in views.py, create them in a file called `__init__.py` that's in a package defined at the top level of your views

Here's a structure:

```
project
   foo
      __init__.py
      bar
         __init__.py
         views.py
      baz
         __init__.py
         views.py
```

in `foo/__init__.py`, you'd

```
from flask import Flask
from flask_ask import Ask

app = Flask(__name__)
ask = Ask(app, '/myendpoint')
```

then in `bar/__init__.py` and `baz/__init__.py`

you can

```
from foo import ask
```

and define routes in the respective files. Let me know if that helps.

The problem is not related with `xmltodict` at all, it's just that you are not building the `OrderedDict`s correctly.

The constructor takes a list of key/value tuples as parameter, but you are passing a `dict` instead, which has already lost the ordering information before the `OrderedDict` is even created. Please have a look at the [official documentation](https://docs.python.org/2/library/collections.html#ordereddict-examples-and-recipes).

Oh, the `--petname=Bob` UI I sketched out has a serious usability problem: you have to decide up-front that this transfer is something that you might want to use again in the future. Worse, both sides have to make that decision (Wormhole Seeds only work in pairs).

We could improve this a tiny bit by sending a flag: if one side uses `--petname=` to remember the Seed, the other side could prompt the user to do the same. `The other person decided to save this connection to later use: do you want to save it too? (Y/n)`. It would still require at least one side to be thinking ahead, though.

Another option is to ask at the end of the transfer. `Transfer complete: do you want to save this connection for future use? (enter petname to save under, or ENTER to skip):`. But asking that every time would make normal use kind of annoying.

A third option is to remember the Wormhole Seed for the most recent transfer in `~/.wormhole/` somewhere, and add a command that retroactively assigns a persistent name to that seed. So `Transfer complete: to save this connection for later use, please run "wormhole save-seed PETNAME" before performing your next wormhole transfer`. It's still the case that both sides have to save it, though.

A more persistent application would have an addressbook, which remembers the Seeds (or more sophisticated non-Wormhole encryption keys) under petnames, and file transfers would use the petnames exclusively. The only use of wormhole codes would be to add new entries to the addressbook. This would be super-useful (and in fact I created magic-wormhole to enable just this sort of application), but I think it's counter to the handy immediacy of the CLI tool. So I think we should continue to aim `wormhole send` as primarily a quick one-off transfer tool, but provide the persistence/repeatability as a secondary option.

ffmpeg sometimes prints warnings like this when the timestamps of subsequent frames are non-monotonously increasing (i.e. going backwards in time or staying the same). This may happen with particular combinations of containers and video/audio streams. In your case it likely happens because the output is not a real container (i.e. `NUL`) – for some codecs you want your output container to be a seekable file, which `NUL` isn't.

This is not currently supported or on the roadmap. Perhaps there is a way to reduce the formulas after fitting the model, but recursing every program looking for the miriad ways that a branch of the program tree could become redundant would probably be equally, or more, computationally expensive than the calculation of the program's output even with redundant branches.

Just to clarify for anyone who runs into this issue in the future. If you get an error saying that `jt` has not been installed correctly when trying to install a theme, first check to make sure the prioritized version of python on your system is included in your `$PYTHONPATH`.

*In a terminal*
```sh
# points to custom python install
$ echo $PYTHONPATH
# path to current prioritized python version
$ which python
```

If `echo $PYTHONPATH` doesn't return anything, open up your shell profile and update your `$PYTHONPATH` to include the one returned by `which python`.

*In your .bashrc (or equivalent)*
```sh
#ex. if which python returned /home/jingyang/anaconda3/bin/python
export PYTHONPATH="/home/vanillaice/anaconda3/bin/python"
```

Save, open a new terminal session, and retry theme install.

@Mortinke My answer to this is similar to my answer to #324 : if you need this, you should use the [Python API](http://awslimitchecker.readthedocs.io/en/latest/python_usage.html) via a wrapper script, such as the [check_aws_limits.py example](https://github.com/jantman/awslimitchecker/blob/master/docs/examples/check_aws_limits.py) (which also has example [unit tests](https://github.com/jantman/awslimitchecker/blob/master/docs/examples/test_check_aws_limits.py) and a [brief overview here](https://github.com/jantman/awslimitchecker/tree/master/docs/examples#check_aws_limitspy)).

IMO, multiple accounts is a bit of a complicated situation and tends to be organization-specific. As an example:

* Do you run with assumed roles, or different static creds, or do you get temporary creds from some service like Vault?
* Who gets notifications for different accounts? The same team, or different teams? How do you determine the contact method/details (i.e. email address, pagerduty service, etc.) for different accounts?
* How do you tell which accounts are production (i.e. send to a pager) and which are dev/test/lab (and only notify via email)?
* How do you know which regions are active and need to be checked in which accounts?

Because of the wide variety of answers to questions like these and more for different users, there isn't official multi-account support built in to awslimitchecker itself.

If you're interested, I can add a multi-account example based on the code that my current employer uses. We run awslimitchecker from Jenkins, one job per account, with per-account limit and threshold overrides, and assumed roles for all but the account that Jenkins runs in. However an example could just as easily run for all accounts and combine the results.

import time, sys
from jsonrpclib.SimpleJSONRPCServer import SimpleJSONRPCServer


class Control:
    def __init__(self):
        self.count = 0
    def SetBPState(self, name, value):
        print "SetBPState",name,value
    
    def GetVOState(self, name):
        print "GetVoState",name
        self.count = self.count+1
        return self.count 
        


class Server:
    def __init__(self, ip, port):
        self.continueloop = True
        self.daemon = None
        self.ip = ip
        self.port = port
        self.ctrl = None
        
    def Loop(self):
        while self.continueloop:
            self.Start()
        
    def Restart(self):
        self.Stop()
    
    def Quit(self):
        self.continueloop = False
        self.Stop()
    
    def Start(self):
        if True:
            self.daemon = SimpleJSONRPCServer((self.ip,self.port))
            self.ctrl = Control()

            # on enregistre les fonctions disponibles via JSON-RPC            
            self.daemon.register_function(self.ctrl.SetBPState,'SetBPState')
            self.daemon.register_function(self.ctrl.GetVOState,'GetVOState')

        
            print "The daemon runs on port :",self.port
            print "The object's uri is : %s" % (self.ip)
            sys.stdout.flush()
            
            self.daemon.serve_forever()

#        except KeyboardInterrupt:
#            self.Quit()
        else:
            print "sleep"
            time.sleep(0.5)
    
    def Stop(self):
        self.daemon.shutdown(True)

```py
# split text into lines
lines = text.split('\n')
# For the first line, try to match `. {lines[0][:75]}`, i.e. the first
# 75 characters of the first line. (75, or linesize - 5) is chosen so
# that it catches lines that are `  1. ` inside a program or for loop
#
# If it's a match, look at child.before to see how many characters were
# matched. If the line had more characters than were matched, take off
# the first 75 characters, prepend `> ` and try to match again.
```

Bellow is an example of how i used the transaction trick, it might be helpful to others:
```python
try:
    with transaction.atomic(savepoint=False):
        # Get the latest version that is approved and before date
        version = Version.objects.get_for_object(club_reg).filter(
            revision__date_created__lte=before_date,
            revision__metadata__approved=True).first()
        # Revert to this version
        version.revision.revert(delete=True)
        # Refresh to get the correct object
        club_reg.refresh_from_db()
        # Serialize or render before raising error to roll back transaction
        serializer = club_reg_serializer.ClubRegReportSerializer(club_reg, context={'request':request})
        data = JSONRenderer().render(serializer.data)
        # Raise the error to roll back the changes
        raise IntegrityError
except IntegrityError:
    # Cache the error to continue as normal
    pass
# Return the data that was already rendered inside the transaction
return data
```

`

print(km.survival_function_)

`
Produces 
`          KM_estimate
timeline             
0.000        1.000000

0.816       1.001     0.000       1.0     0.000    1.993
0.990       0.992     0.992       0.0     0.000    0.992
`
as would be expected. However, if we make a slight change to the index of the input dataframe
`df = pd.DataFrame(index=[5,6,7,8])

Could I have some pointers on using the "bus" pattern?

I tried to emulate the example at https://github.com/dysinger/nanomsg-examples#bus, but it's not working quite right.

I establish a `BUS` socket, wait a moment (so I can manually start the other instances), and then connect to the other instances. After connection, we emit a message, and then go into a receive loop. For some reason, I receive two of every message from the other nodes.

This is written in Python, but it should be intuitive, and this is [most likely] a problem that's caused by me not the Python library:

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3

import time
import argparse
import pickle
import collections

import nanomsg

# Message types.

_MT_HELLO = 'hello'
_MT_HELLO_RESPONSE = 'hello_response'

# Miscellaneous constants.

_DESCRIPTION = "Establish a node on a bus"

# Types

_MESSAGE = collections.namedtuple(
            '_MESSAGE',
            ['type', 'node', 'text'])

def _parse_args():
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description=_DESCRIPTION)

    parser.add_argument(
        'name',
        help='The name of this node')

    parser.add_argument(
        'node_uri',
        help='The URI to bind for this node')

    parser.add_argument(
        '-p', '--peer-uri',
        action='append',
        default=[],
        help='The URI of a peer to connect to')

    args = parser.parse_args()

    return args

def _build_message(type_name, node_name, text_template, *args):
    text = text_template % args

    return pickle.dumps((type_name, node_name, text))

def _parse_message(encoded_message):
    parts = pickle.loads(encoded_message)
    return _MESSAGE(*parts)

def _process_messages(s, node_name):
    print("Entering receive loop.")

    while 1:
        encoded_message = s.recv()
        message = _parse_message(encoded_message)

        print("Received message of type [%s] from node [%s]: [%s]" % 
              (message.type, message.node, message.text))

def _start_server(node_name, node_uri, peer_uri_list):
    print("Starting server: [%s] [%s]" % (node_name, node_uri))

    s = nanomsg.Socket(nanomsg.BUS)

    # Configure the local port.

    s.bind(node_uri)

    # Impose a delay to allow the other instances to get to this point.

    print("Bound. Waiting to connect.")
    time.sleep(10)

    # Connect to other nodes.

    for i, peer_uri in enumerate(peer_uri_list):
        print("Connecting (%d): %s" % (i, peer_uri))
        s.connect(peer_uri)

    # Impose a delay to allow the other nodes to connect.

    print("Waiting for all connections to complete.")
    time.sleep(5)

    # Send initial message.

    print("Sending hello.")

    encoded_message = \
        _build_message(
            _MT_HELLO, 
            node_name, 
            'Hello.')

    s.send(encoded_message)

    try:
        _process_messages(s, node_name)
    finally:
        s.close()

if __name__ == '__main__':
    args = _parse_args()
    _start_server(args.name, args.node_uri, args.peer_uri)
```

I've finished the refactoring now, available on this branch https://github.com/jrief/django-sass-processor/tree/media-processor

I've never worked with Jinja2, so please excuse my missing knowledge.

In Django templates you pass in the path of the scss file in the constructor of ``SassSrcNode``, while for me it seems as if in Jinja2 this path is passed in while rendering the templatetag.

I now refactored the code into a ``SassProcessor`` doing the heavy lifting and a much smaller ``SassSrcNode`` doing the template tag stuff. This allows me to reuse the ``SassProcessor`` outside of  templatetags, for instance in media classes and properties, as used by Django admin.

Please have a look at the branch. I believe it should be quite simple to re-add Jinja2 support again. Sorry about taking so long to respond. I think I understand why you want to do this, but you haven't dealt with all the impacts of raising a new exception. I suggest you do the following:

1. Put my test back in from PR #16. (It failed when I tried to run it with the code from this branch.)
2. Look at all the other places where you call `get_attribute()` and add exception handlers.

Let me know if you'd like me to help with this.

In the exception handlers, you'll probably need to distinguish between [`ObjectDoesNotExist` exceptions][ex] and other `AttributeError` exceptions, because `ObjectDoesNotExist` exceptions should be used for filtering, where other `AttributeError` exceptions should be raised to the caller. (My test on the other pull request just uses a plain `AttributeError`, so it would need to be changed.)

Here's a snippet that shows two different attribute requests against a real database that will raise a `DoesNotExist` exception and a plain `AttributeError` exception. (I'm working on some features that will raise these two errors from a mocked model, but I'm not ready to create a pull request for them yet.)

[ex]: https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.10/ref/exceptions/#django.core.exceptions.ObjectDoesNotExist

    # Tested with Django 1.9.2
    import logging
    import sys
    
    import django
    from django.apps import apps
    from django.apps.config import AppConfig
    from django.conf import settings
    from django.core.exceptions import ObjectDoesNotExist
    from django.db import connections, models, DEFAULT_DB_ALIAS
    from django.db.models.base import ModelBase
    
    from mock import patch, PropertyMock, MagicMock
    
    NAME = 'udjango'
    
    
    def main():
    
        setup()
    
        class Person(models.Model):
            first_name = models.CharField(max_length=30)
            last_name = models.CharField(max_length=40)
    
        class Personality(models.Model):
            person = models.OneToOneField(Person, related_name="personality")
            description = models.CharField(max_length=1000)
    
            def __str__(self):
                return self.description
    
        syncdb(Person)
        syncdb(Personality)
    
        p1 = Person(first_name='Jimmy', last_name='Jones')
        p1.save()
        p2 = Person(first_name='Bob', last_name='Brown')
        p2.save()
        Personality.objects.create(person=p1, description='peppy')
    
        logging.info("Jimmy's personality is %s.", p1.personality.description)
        try:
            logging.info("Bob's personality is %s.", p2.personality.description)
        except Personality.DoesNotExist:
            logging.info("Bob has no personality.")
    
        try:
            logging.info("Bob's bogus field is %s.", p2.bogus_field)
        except AttributeError:
            logging.info("Bob has no bogus field.")
    
    
    def setup():
        DB_FILE = NAME + '.db'
        with open(DB_FILE, 'w'):
            pass  # wipe the database
        settings.configure(
            DEBUG=True,
            DATABASES={
                DEFAULT_DB_ALIAS: {
                    'ENGINE': 'django.db.backends.sqlite3',
                    'NAME': DB_FILE}},
            LOGGING={'version': 1,
                     'disable_existing_loggers': False,
                     'formatters': {
                        'debug': {
                            'format': '%(asctime)s[%(levelname)s]'
                                      '%(name)s.%(funcName)s(): %(message)s',
                            'datefmt': '%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S'}},
                     'handlers': {
                        'console': {
                            'level': 'DEBUG',
                            'class': 'logging.StreamHandler',
                            'formatter': 'debug'}},
                     'root': {
                        'handlers': ['console'],
                        'level': 'INFO'},
                     'loggers': {
                        "django.db": {"level": "DEBUG"}}})
        app_config = AppConfig(NAME, sys.modules['__main__'])
        apps.populate([app_config])
        django.setup()
        original_new_func = ModelBase.__new__
    
        @staticmethod
        def patched_new(cls, name, bases, attrs):
            if 'Meta' not in attrs:
                class Meta:
                    app_label = NAME
                attrs['Meta'] = Meta
            return original_new_func(cls, name, bases, attrs)
        ModelBase.__new__ = patched_new
    
    
    def syncdb(model):
        """ Standard syncdb expects models to be in reliable locations.
    
        Based on https://github.com/django/django/blob/1.9.3
        /django/core/management/commands/migrate.py#L285
        """
        connection = connections[DEFAULT_DB_ALIAS]
        with connection.schema_editor() as editor:
            editor.create_model(model)
    
    main()

What is bothering me a bit is that a lot of projects, including Zinnia, are favoring the latest Django release over the LTS version 1.8. Okay, I just realized that I am doing the whole thing in an unnecessarily complicated way. I don't need to modify the data dictionary of the entire existing Project object. I can use partial=True style update and submit only the newly added issue:

```
p = Project.objects.all().first()
ser = ProjectSerializer(instance=p)
new_issue = {'events': [{'status': 'In Progress'}]}
ser2 = ProjectSerializer(instance=p, data=new_issue, partial=True)
if ser2.is_valid():
    ser2.save
else:
    print ser2.errors
```

This works and is much more concise. It would still be nice if dictionary items set to None didn't trigger "required" validation error when the corresponding field is set to required=False, but the "cleaner" way to do it is to avoid None values in the first place. Thanks!

1. Yes, in general, inferencing at serving time would not have the labels.  That said, for "validation" and "test" sets, you have labels s.t. you can gauge accuracy of your model (independent of the "training" set).  So this example's "inference" path is really just doing validation/test of the model, but you can modify the code as necessary for true (unlabeled) inferencing.

2. I don't think that setting the `--batch_size` will do what you want here.  Basically, Spark Streaming has an interval for which it will wait to collect incoming records.  When that interval expires, a mini RDD is sent to your application code.  At that point, you application code can consume the RDD data in whatever fashion it wants.  In this case, processing `--batch_size` items at a time.  However, in this case, the mini RDD will still only arrive every 10 seconds.  If you need true single-item prediction serving, you probably should consider using something like [TF-Serving](https://www.tensorflow.org/serving/), which is the TF-recommended way to do this...

There appear to be a few questions and misconceptions here around API keys and security.

**base64**

Firstly, @bradwbonn remember base64 doesn't make any difference to security. It's trivially readable by a human -- base64 is _not_ hashing, it's just a simple transform. Credentials stored as base64 are as secure as those stored as separate plain text username/password pairs, because that's what they are. I suggest storing the plain credentials, because it's easier for others and your future self to understand what the security properties of the script are (if the server's insecure, you've bigger issues anyway :) )

I *think* this negates the need for accessing the authorization header in your case; but clearly it could be useful for other scenarios, such as authenticating against a proxy server rather than Cloudant itself. I think our workaround works for now, but I do quite like @vinomaster's suggestion -- though it appears `requests`-specific (though I can't imagine changing!).

**Setting permissions**

To set the security on a database from an API key and password, you'll need to set up the `_security` document. However, looks like you can't do that yet from issue https://github.com/cloudant/python-cloudant/issues/52.

**Using API keys with python-cloudant**

@vinomaster I think your question is around using API keys; let me know if I misunderstood, there's a bunch of issues here :) 

To use an API key and password, it's important to note that they are just username and password pairs, so you use them as follows (feel free to try it as-is, the key is a valid read key for that db at least for the next few days):

```python
USERNAME = "bouninamendouldnimendepa"
PASSWORD = "e6fda548ce40d21ae675d03068bb0f913f2d99f1"
ACCOUNT_NAME = "mikerhodes"
DATABASE_NAME = "animaldb"
DOC_NAME = "badger"

from cloudant.account import Cloudant

client = Cloudant(USERNAME, PASSWORD, account=ACCOUNT_NAME)

# Connect to the account and establish a session cookie
client.connect()
session = client.session()

database = client[DATABASE_NAME]

document = database[DOC_NAME]
print "Got document: ", document

client.disconnect()
```

As @bradwbonn said, this appears to not be working with the context manager, that is, this fails:

```python
# With context manager
with Cloudant(USERNAME, PASSWORD, account=ACCOUNT_NAME) as client:
    session = client.session()
    database = client[DATABASE_NAME]
    document = database[DOC_NAME]
    print "Got document from context manager: ", document
```

I filed this as issue https://github.com/cloudant/python-cloudant/issues/53.

I actually think we do the right thing here, which is to start 6 separate traces (one for the parent, and one for each child). I've modified the example to demonstrate:

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
import threading
import sys
from py_zipkin.logging_helper import zipkin_logger
from py_zipkin.thread_local import get_thread_local_zipkin_attrs
from py_zipkin.zipkin import zipkin_span

def main():
    with zipkin_span(service_name='main', span_name='kicking_off_threads', transport_handler=handler, sample_rate=100):
        sys.stderr.write("parent thread: {}\n".format(get_thread_local_zipkin_attrs()))
        threads = []
        for i in range(5):
            t = threading.Thread(target=test_threading)
            threads.append(t)
            t.start()

def handler(message):
    pass

@zipkin_span(service_name='test_threading', span_name='test_threading', transport_handler=handler, sample_rate=100)
def test_threading():
    sys.stderr.write("child thread [{}]: {}\n".format(threading.current_thread().ident, get_thread_local_zipkin_attrs()))
    return

main()
```

Hmm, sounds fishy.  We don't have git specific logic, but we do have this bit of logic:

https://github.com/facebook/watchman/blob/a1bd09087d6070f6dfcecd98c3a1a08dea4c2f40/root/vcs.cpp#L52-L55

The idea is that we want to place our sync cookies in a directory that doesn't participate in the SCM status operation in case we get killed and leave behind a junk file in the repo.  We do this based on the vcs_ignore_dirs setting; this is just a list of dirs that we partially ignore (we watch only its direct contents, but not anything lower).

We should only pick one of those dirs if it shows up as a dir, and that should make that "not a directory" error impossible.

However, we do have this long standing issue that surfaced from an earlier attempt at integrating watchman with git: https://github.com/facebook/watchman/issues/25

Is it possible that that path in the error message was originally a nested git repo and that it was later swapped out for a subrepo?

Thanks, indeed it is a feature request.

### The story of the use case:
I want to control my leds, etc. in my room and I am searching for an offline (linux) solution to accomplish this. I am not willing to send everything to the cloud, not even on demand.

My current solution is snowboy hotword detection which recognizes a generic hotword (like alexa) and then recognizes a personalized hotword. It works good, but not best, as all words are recognize independent from each other.

Mozilla deepspeech does not work for me, as it does not properly recognize my voice, possibly due to the "not so perfect" microphone or other factors. I used the provided sound models etc.

Then I tried the microsoft speech recognition (note: there are 3 apis available, of which only [this](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/uwp/api/Windows.Media.SpeechRecognition) works properly for me). I built it using the example from [here](https://www.hackster.io/krvarma/rpivoice-051857) and combined it with the sample further down [this page](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/uwp/design/input/speech-recognition).

### The use case
This speech recognition works perfect for a specified [grammar](https://github.com/krvarma/RPiVoice/blob/master/src/RPiVoice/Grammar/grammar.xml). You can say "turn on green LED" or "turn off bedroom lights". It only recognizes word within the grammar, this way it is more precise and will not recognize "right" instead of "light".

The Microsoft speech API also outputs a confidence level (many other APIs do that as well). If you filter out all "low" hits and only recognize "medium" and "high" recognition results you have a very decent voice detection.

If this grammar mechanism could be applied to deepspeech I hope it will get even better at recognizing predefined commands for machine interaction. It will not be used for dictating, but rather for a very precise control of leds/car/etc with a known command set(aka grammar).

The ``connection_made`` in the ``ProtocolConsumer`` is fired when the ``Connection`` (the protocol) creates a new consumer, i.e. when it needs to build the consumer to consume data received from the connection transport.

Therefore, simply connecting won't fire and ``connection_made`` event on the ``ProtocolConsumer`` but it will fire a ``connection_made`` event on the Connection.

Try sending a message and you should see the event fired.

To have ``connection_made`` event on the Connection, you need to add handler to it rather than the ``ProtocolConsumer``.

The data model revolves around citations which we call articles.  The article ID is our only unique constraint.  Each article has an ID, name, description, source, publication date, etc.  In addition we have other nodes that each article might be associated with.  They include author, grant, chemicals, and journals.  We first create the articles, authors, grants, chemicals and journal nodes.  Next we re-read the citations and create relationships from the articles to the associated authors, grants, chemicals, and journals.  We use merge() instead of create() because we so often need to restart the ingestion and this permits us to initially ingest the nodes as well as update them later using the same class.  We did try create() at some point and did not get the performance increase we were expecting.

i never really designed annoy to care about holes – it's been working somewhat unintentionally.

should be possible to filter them out on index building pretty easily. look at https://github.com/spotify/annoy/blob/master/src/annoylib.h#L526 – all you need to do there is to ignore any vector that's all zeros.
On #2: You don't *have* to move the root nodes at the end of the index. It's nice if you do. But they only take up a very small amount of memory.
the other solution is just put it in a separate file and use some existing hashmap format like bdb, TC,

Annoy doesn't guarantee to find the nearest items – that's suspected to be an NP complete problem. It will return most of them though.

Try to increase the parameter `search_k` to something larger. That will cause Annoy to search more nodes.

With only 13000 items it's possible you might be better off just doing an exhaustive search.

The other option I would recommend is to pre-process the data set by doing dimensionality reduction. Annoy was built for roughly <100 dimensions, although I've seen it work well up to 1000 dimensions. Not sure what distribution your data set has, but something like a truncated SVD might work well: http://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.decomposition.TruncatedSVD.html

I've created/use something with a very similar purpose as slyons implementation.  An example is if I want to run a pipeline that has 3 tasks: Task 1 reads data, Task 2 processes it using a ML algorithm developed within spark, Task 3 writes/handles the output, Task 4 runs arbitary sparkSQL(file on HDFS) on the output.  If I want to run all of this using luigi within one pyspark context, I don't think this is doable as luigi currently stands (You'd have to submit 4 independent spark apps).  

The alternate implementation I use is [here](https://github.com/Benyuel/luigi-spark) for reference.  It has other modified features, so it's not solely for this purpose above.   This implementation handles submitting spark jobs programmatically via luigi and those spark jobs can be luigi tasks as well.  Once you submit a luigi task within a pyspark context, the modification in that repo allows arbitrary workers (cluster nodes running the spark job on a yarn cluster) to connect to my central scheduler and handle dependencies, resources, etc,. as a 'normal' luigi task would do outside of spark.  It's a little convoluted, but hopefully that makes sense.

Thanks for the nice writeup. That really helped! I am able to reproduce this as well. Here is what I did:

1) Create a csv file with this script:
```python
input_file = 'myinput.csv'
num_rows = 1024 * 1024 * 50


with open(input_file, 'w') as f:
    for i in range(num_rows):
        f.write('%s\n' % i)
```

2) Upload it with the CLI:
```
$ aws s3 cp myinput.csv s3://mybucketfoo/
```

3) Run this script that just downloads the file in append mode:
```python
import boto3
bucket           = 'mybucketfoo' 
file_key         = 'myinput.csv' 
output_file_path = 'myoutput.csv'

s3     = boto3.resource('s3')
bucket = s3.Bucket(bucket)

#Dump binary in append mode
with open(output_file_path, 'ab') as file_object:
    bucket.download_fileobj(
        Key     = file_key, 
        Fileobj = file_object,
    )
```

4) Run an MD5 on it:
```
$ md5 myinput.csv myoutput.csv
MD5 (myinput.csv) = 6122e1db4d4fda33e49fe7e53690db52
MD5 (myoutput.csv) = a5bc1d5b2e4d67b1ec62bea227589ea4
```
As you can see by the final step, we get mismatching MD5's. We are going to have to do some digging to figure out why this is the case. I have a feeling it has to do with the combination of threading being used and the append mode. Thanks for reporting though!

I too am having trouble 100% characterizing this problem.  When I try a manual setup (in python3 command line interpreter) and try logging.info() of 10 million character message it doesn't have a problem.
Now there are a few differences with my manual try and the error case in boto3:
1. Not running inside Flask.
2. Not running with our logging redirector that writes to a queue.

Our uwsgi service redirects to our own logging service that writes to RabbitMQ.
We sometimes saw the "BlockingIOError: [Errno 11] write could not complete without blocking" message, so I suspect our logging redirector caps at 16K and sends back an error and then we get "OSError: [Errno 90] Message too long" coming back to the boto3-python process.

Overall other customers might not encounter this problem because they probably log differently.
Maybe the best that could be done is to have those 3 locations inside action.py to have some reasonable (but large) capping of the log message - because other customers may run into this in the future.

-----------
Our code runs inside of Flask uwsgi (fronted by apache).
We use our own JSONLoggingFormatter object for formatting our log mesages, but it just does a json.dumps:
```
class JSONLoggingFormatter(logging.Formatter):
    MAP_PYTHON_LEVEL_TO_LVL = {logging.CRITICAL: 'FATAL', logging.ERROR: 'ERROR',
        logging.WARNING: 'WARN', logging.INFO: 'INFO', logging.DEBUG: 'DEBUG'}

    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        logging.Formatter.__init__(self, *args, **kwargs)
        self._fqdn = socket.getfqdn()

    def format(self, record):
        fields = {}
        fields.update({
            'hst': self._fqdn,
            'lvl': self.MAP_PYTHON_LEVEL_TO_LVL.get(record.levelno),
            # Use the base logging.Formatter in order to format any exceptions properly.
            'msg': logging.Formatter.format(self, record),
            'pid': record.process,
            'tid': record.thread,
            # Python does not include the ISO8601 Z suffix when formatting a naive datetime
            # so explicitly include it.
            'tsi': datetime.datetime.utcfromtimestamp(record.created).isoformat() + 'Z'
        })
        return json.dumps(fields, separators=(',', ':'))

out_hdlr = logging.StreamHandler(sys.stdout)
out_hdlr.setFormatter(JSONLoggingFormatter())
out_hdlr.setLevel(logging.INFO)
logger = logging.getLogger()
logger.addHandler(out_hdlr)
logger.setLevel(logging.INFO)
```

btw for asyncio I found I could do it by serializing the parent span in the base process via:

```python
        dd_span = tracer.get_call_context().get_current_span()
        attrs = {'name', 'service', 'resource', 'span_type', 'trace_id', 'span_id', 'parent_id', 'start'}
        dd_span_info = {n: getattr(dd_span, n) for n in attrs} if dd_span else None
```

then smashing it in in the child process:

```python
try:
        if dd_span_info:
            dd_ctx = tracer.get_call_context()
            parent_span = Span(tracer, **dd_span_info, context=dd_ctx)

            dd_ctx._current_span = parent_span

            task = asyncio.Task.current_task(loop=self._loop)
            set_call_context(task, dd_ctx)
finally:
            if dd_span_info:
                set_call_context(task, None)
```

    def __init__(self, host, port, username, password, datacenter): #, heart_beat=180

    def get_vm_resource(self, vm_name, vm_folder=None, is_template=False):
        """
        获取虚拟机的资源及配置信息，
        主要包括：cpu核数、内存大小、mac地址、存储空间大小（单位GB）、ip地址、开机状态等信息

        vm_resource_info = {}
        net_conf_info = []
        content, error_code, error_msg = self._connect_vcenter()
        # content, error_code, error_msg = self._refresh_connection()
        if error_code:
            return None, error_code, error_msg
        ret, error_code, error_msg = self._center_location(content)
        if error_code:
            return None, error_code, error_msg
        vm, error_code, error_msg = self._vm_location(ret, vm_name, vm_folder, is_template=is_template)
        if not vm:
            vm, error_code, error_msg = self._vm_location(ret, vm_name, vm_folder=u"已发现虚拟机", is_template=is_template)
            if error_code:
                return None, error_code, error_msg
        os_info = (vm.summary.config.guestFullName).split("(")
        for item in vm.guest.net:
            if isinstance(item, vim.vm.GuestInfo.NicInfo):
                if "Microsoft" in os_info[0]:
                    dhcp = item.ipConfig.dhcp.ipv4.enable if item.ipConfig else False
                else:
                    dhcp = False
                dns_list = list(dns for dns in item.dnsConfig.ipAddress) if item.dnsConfig else None
                if item.ipConfig:
                    for ip_info in item.ipConfig.ipAddress:
                        if "." in ip_info.ipAddress:
                            ip_addr = ip_info.ipAddress
                            net_conf_info.append(dict(ip_addr=ip_addr, dns=dns_list, mac_addr=item.macAddress, status=item.connected, dhcp=dhcp))
        vm_disk_list = list(int(item.capacityInKB / 1024 / 1024)for item in vm.config.hardware.device if isinstance(item, vim.vm.device.VirtualDisk))
        vm_disk_capacity = reduce(lambda x, y: x + y, vm_disk_list)
        vm_resource_info.update(
            os_name=os_info[0],
            bit=os_info[1][:2],
            status=vm.summary.runtime.powerState,
            memory=vm.summary.config.memorySizeMB,
            cpu_num=vm.summary.config.numCpu,
            disk_size=vm_disk_capacity,
            net_conf_info=net_conf_info,
            is_template=vm.config.template,
            annotation=vm.summary.config.annotation
            )

        return vm_resource_info, 0, 0

@dainnilsson 
>Is the reason for adding the `pin` argument to sign, rather than explicitly calling verify() before calling sign(), due to verify() also calling authenticate() in some circumstances (making the verification unusable for a PIN policy of ALWAYS)?

Yes, that's the main reason. I also thought it would make sense that if signing might require PIN verification, then the sign method could accept an optional PIN parameter.

>Would having PivController.verify() call the actual VERIFY instruction again at the end, if it called authenticate(), solve the problem?

Yes, that is an alternative (and much smaller) solution. I think the reason why I dind't go with that one is because I came at the problem from the other direction and discovered this after the fact.

>I think it makes the API a bit weird having sign take a pin argument which it doesn't use unless it gets an error. This means that the call may succeed even if the PIN is wrong.

This is a very good point. I agree, then, that staying with separate calls to `verify()` and `sign()` is the way to go.

>Generally I would prefer to catch the error at a higher level, and THEN prompt the user for the PIN in the case where it's needed, rather than preemptively doing so.

I'll note that you can still do this by not sending a `pin` argument to `sign()`, and then prompting for PIN and retrying, but it does kind of defeat the purpose of the `pin` parameter.

It would be super useful for speeding up CI builds if `sync` had a flag to exit if everything is in sync.   A lot of SaaS CI platforms include code caches such as Travis, AWS codebuild, Bitbucket Pipelines, etc. The code cache is restored at the beginning of a run and if the dependencies didn't require updates it could shave 30s or more off of the build.

As it is `pipenv sync --dev` takes about 30s with a private build. It contains 100 or so deps in total. If `pipenv verify` (or whatever) had a return code of `1` on "out of sync" and `0` when it's "in sync".

Basically it would allow me to write something like this;

```
pipenv verify --dev
if [ $? -eq 1 ]; then
pipenv sync --dev
fi
```

If pipenv did the above internally that would be even better!

The point isn't that we know we need to keep a specific version pinned, nor that we don't want to update sometime soon.  We just don't want to be forced to update at the same time we switch to pipenv, because pipenv is supposed to be *better* at helping us to keep packages up to date while retaining the ability to manage the upgrades with care!  In fact, even if literally the next commit I make is to upgrade all the packages, I'd like to do that upgrade separately, so I know, when inevitably something breaks, whether it was setting things up incorrectly when switching to pipenv or updating the packages.  (And, depending how careful I'm being, I may want to do that separately for each package.)

Perhaps the better way to do that is to simply leave each the version pin in the Pipfile until we're ready to update that package?  But then we lose the ability to use e.g. `pipenv update --dry-run` to see what we're going to update -- and it seems like this is not the best way for a Pipfile+Pipfile.lock to express what we want, and is different from what we would have if we had used pipenv from the start.

Can't setuptools support requirement specifiers (environment markers) in `install_requires`? There should be no need for `extras_require` when you specify them in `install_requires`.

## pip works, setuptools breaks

I have a `requirements.txt` file in every project, which is also read by the project's `setup.py`. Hence, my users / developers can choose to run `python setup.py install` (`develop`) or run `pip install -r requirements.txt`. Which ever comes more natural to them.

Unfortunately, using [environment markers](http://pip.readthedocs.org/en/stable/reference/pip_install/#requirement-specifiers) is supported by `pip` but not by `setuptools`:

Using pip (with Python 3): 
``` bash
$ pip install -r requirements.txt 
Ignoring dnspython: markers "python_version < '3'" don't match your environment
Installing collected packages: dnspython3
Successfully installed dnspython3-1.12.0
```

Using setuptools (with Python 3): 
``` bash
$ python setup.py develop
error in GuuruBackoffice setup command: 'install_requires' must be a string or list of strings containing valid project/version requirement specifiers; Expected version spec in dnspython3 ;python_version >= '3' at  ;python_version >= '3'
```

## Related code samples

My `requirements.txt` file:
``` python
dnspython3 ;python_version >= '3'
dnspython ;python_version < '3'
```
My `setup.py` script:
``` python
def read_file(filename):
    with open(join(abspath(dirname(__file__)), filename)) as f:
        return f.read()

setup(
    # ...
    install_requires=read('requirements.txt'),
    # ...
)
```

@cdeil My motivation to define the polygon using *relative* positions (e.g. from zero) instead of *absolute* positions was for aperture photometry.  In other words, the *shape* of the polygon can be defined by the *relative* positions.  That would be like a `PolygonAperture` object.  The `PolygonAperture` object could then be applied to the data at different absolute positions.

If the region is defined using *absolute* vertices, I would need to redefine a new `PolygonAperture` for every new position it is to be applied to the data.

Yes, NuPIC can be run in a clustered way, but there is a lot of manual work to do. We (Numenta) have run NuPIC in the past in a clustered SaaS platform. We used regular model serialization to database to keep state over time. When nodes dropped out, they were replaced and re-instantiated with the same nodes. 

It was not easy to build, and it will not be easy to maintain. Also, not something that is in scope of NuPIC work sponsored by Numenta. :confused: 

But! I think this is a product that could be built out. When we had this SaaS service years ago, I thought it was great. It was a REST API so you could create models, swarm, pass data, get predictions, etc. It had user accounts and collections. It was always on and redundant. Unfortunately, this code is legacy and was not open-sourced. It had some architectural problems, too. The community is working on a very small HTTP REST API for NuPIC called [HTM in the Cloud (HITC)](https://github.com/nupic-community/hitc), but it is in its infancy and has not clustering ability, scalability, or anything really. But it could at least provide a place to start. @jonnoFTW has been working on it.

Just for info the results of my further experiments with this:

* Threading is definitely unrelated, setting the gevent resolver to ares disabled any threading, but the problem persisted
* Problem not demonstrateable with a freshly initialized (nearly empty) database
* Problem not demonstrateable with direct in-process FileStorage access
* Problem still present after packing the database
* Problem also happens with elder versions, tested with 3.10.5 and recent versions
* Parallel to the segfault, there are errors with keys not found in the BTree, which are supposed to be there. This may or may not be related (again not present on direct FileStorage access)
* ZEO server process never reports any error
* It seems easier reproducible, the more greenlets are accessing and modifying the database in parallel, ranging from not reproducible with maybe less than 50 greenlets to nearly instant with 150+. Though, none of them are creating heavy load, just infrequently modifying a handful of objects each
* All my tries to write a slim and simple reproducer failed so far

I'm afraid this isn't too helpful yet, so I'll have some more tries in getting something reproducible together. My thoughts so far, please correct me if I'm drawing wrong conclusions: 

* Data corruption seems unlikely, as the problem persists after the packing, and as the in-process FileStorage access works fine
* There must be some rare? edge case, when BTree gets different data through ClientStorage/ZEOServer than directly from FileStorage, which causes the segfault in _BTree_get

Short answer: in this case assert_array_almost_equal_nulp would be the better choice, with nulp=8 allowing the last three bits to differ.  Or even 4.

Looking a bit closer, this is a linear system, so effectively it is the same algorithm, though one code path goes through BLAS and the other is presumably cython (haven't looked at the polyval implementation).  Depending on exact register usage and processor (Intel processors keep a guard bit on numbers retained in registers that can be discarded if the value is written to memory). Regardless, since the test has been passing on my machines using floating point equality (linux, windows and mac but all x86), that's a pretty good hint that the values should be the same except for the last bit or so.

The all_close test is using 7 digits, which is suitable for single precision, but is way too loose for this case.  There are some cases where that much precision is lost compared to an analytic solution (e.g., optimizers may stop when df is less than 1e-7), but not here.  In general, I will adjust a test to use as many bits as I can get away with, sometimes adjusting the algorithm to make the results more precise.

As I mentioned, virtually everyone other than VS < 2013 supports `_Bool` (and `stdbool.h`), **even in C89 mode**.  With what I suggested Brotli would still be completely usable in your projects.

Sticking to pure C89 hare isn't really about portability, it's about being technically C89 compliant.  In practice the only place you're likely to run into a real portability problem is VS < 2013, and I suggested a solution for that which would work automatically.

There is a tiny group of people who really need pure C89.  If you're using gcc, clang, msvc >= 12.0, icc, suncc, xlc, or really any other vaguely modern non-toy compiler, *you are not in this group*.  This group consists of people using 20-year-old compilers, or targeting something exotic where only a specific compiler will work and that compiler only supports C89 (may be true for some DSPs or something).  You're more likely to end up on a platform where endianness cannot be determined at compile-time than to have what I proposed be a real portability issue.  However, even for that tiny group of people, what I proposed would only require defining a macro or two.

However, since it sounds like @eustas wants to stick with the `BROTLI_BOOL` thing, I'd suggest something like

```c
#if !defined(BROTLI_BOOL)
#  if (defined(__STDC_VERSION__) && __STDC_VERSION__ >= 199901L)) || \
       (defined(__GNUC__) && (__GNUC__ >= 3)) || \
       (defined(_MSC_VER) && (_MSC_VER >= 1800))
#    include <stdbool.h>
#    define BROTLI_BOOL bool
#  else
#    define BROTLI_BOOL unsigned char
#  endif
#endif
#define BROTLI_TRUE 1
#define BROTLI_FALSE 0
```

Almost every compiler out there pretends to be either GCC or MSVC, and sufficiently recent versions of both are whitelisted as supporting `stdbool.h`, as are compilers in C99+ mode.  Other compilers (mostly old MSVC) will fall back on using `unsigned char`, but for the rare cases where that wouldn't be the correct type people can just define `BROTLI_BOOL` to something which works before including Brotli.

Note that this would be quite easy to adapt to use `bool`, `true`, and `false` instead of creating a separate type for Brotli; you would just have to guard against redefining true and false.  That would give you a much nicer API.

There are two problematic aspects to the normalizations done by Point.

* Using % to do the modulo calculations potentially loses 1 bit of
  accuracy.  E.g., `longitude = 180.000000000000028` becomes `-180`
  instead of `-179.999999999999972`.  GeographicLib uses `math.fmod`
  instead, although this is slightly verbose:

      def AngNormalize(x):
        """reduce angle to (-180,180]"""

        y = math.fmod(x, 360)
        # On Windows 32-bit with python 2.7, math.fmod(-0.0, 360) = +0.0
        # This fixes this bug.
        y = x if x == 0 else y
        return (y + 360 if y <= -180 else
                (y if y <= 180 else y - 360))
      AngNormalize = staticmethod(AngNormalize)

* `latitude = 90.001` which most people would think of as being in the
  vicinity of the north pole becomes `latitude = -89.999`, close to the
  south pole.  I think this would come as a shock to most people.  There
  are various alternatives for latitude = 90.001, longitude = 10:

  1.  raise an exception;
  2. `(NaN, 10)`, i.e., silently mark it as bogus;
  3. `(90, 10)`, i.e., "correct" the latitude;
  4. `(89,999, -170)`, i.e., switch to the antimeridian (but the azimuth
    should also be fixed in this case).

  I suggest i or ii.

As for your point about using the current code on master and just remove the MAP step when before sampling, the answer is that the whole model_return_t_alpha_beta() function simply won't work at all without my hack. If I didn't handle NaNs coming from index discrepancies in the returns series versus the chosen benchmark, the whole model would simply fall over even in calling np.linalg.lstsq(X, y)[0]. In other words, this isn't about MAP but a data issue that has repercussions throughout the code. > as I recall you support both astropy fits and fitsio.

GalSim doesn't actually use fitsio ever, just pyfits or astropy.  You're remembering TreeCorr where I supported both.  Although with the latest version I switching to just fitsio, since it's faster than astropy, and it's simple enough to have setup.py handle the dependency for people.  I haven't made the switch here for GalSim, although maybe someday...

> with fitsio I recommend using a with block to avoid the file closing problem, rather than finally, when it is possible to do so

We do use `with` blocks most of the time when we open files, although we had missed a couple, which I've now added.  In the `galsim.fits.py` routines, our desire to support reading from both a file (opening it for the user) or an open HDUList means that a `with` block doesn't really work (or at least I don't know how to make it work easily).  That's where the `finally` block was helpful.

The batching was done for performance reasons - initially there were no batches, meaning that each row was encoded as TSV and immediately yielded by the generator. This meant that to insert a million rows, gen() was called a million times. This overhead made things pretty slow.
I added the batching, so now when inserting a million rows gen() is called only 1000 times, and this improved performance considerably.

Started investigating the migration to a single-source version aligning with the strategy used in the Python web apps repositories - see https://github.com/ome/omero-figure/pull/215.

The main problem for all Python packages depending on `yaclifw` (`scc` and `omego` afaik) is the `Version` command which internally heavily `git tag` and even rewrites the version. Distribute can't mix after each "to" well dispense as it would mix the contents of each "to" well with the remaining "from" well volume. 

If you want to mix the last well that you want to dispense to, I'd recommend adding a separate mix step at the end.

Alternatively, if you do want to mix between all your "to" wells, you can use:

```
transfer([A, B, C D], [B, C, D, E], [vol*4, vol*3, vol*2, vol], one_source=False, one_tip=True, **mix_kwargs)
```

There's a lot going on here. Sorry I didn't notice it for all these months.

There's a few things to keep in mind which make working with HTTP & Kerberos confusing.

First, I don't believe there is an actual standard API for working with kerberos. They're all implementation specific. MIT & Heimdal are similar, but not identical. which is why people tend to write against GSSAPI instead of directly against kerberos.

GSSAPI is a generic API that works with pluggable authentication mechanisms. One of the (and possibly the most comment) mechanisms is Kerberos. This is why most of the methods we use in the (confusingly named) kerberos library include gssapi in their name.

One problem with using GSSAPI as an abstraction over Kerberos is that while Kerberos has a concept of a REALM (the @INT.EXAMPLE.ORG in your code), GSSAPI does not. That's why you don't see realm names in anything in this library. There's no way to infer it -- your kerberos libraries (MIT/Heimdal) will assume the service is in the same realm as your tgt, and send the request to your realms kdc for (by convention) service/hostname@YOURREALM. The kdc will then check to see if the service is present in its database, and respond if it is with a service ticket, and if not, it may issue a referral to another realm (if cross-realm trust has been established).

Another thing that causes confusion is the similarity between a gss name and a kerberos principal. A gss nt hostbased service takes the form of service@hostname, in this case, the service is HTTP (and no, I can't explain why I made the service configurable in this library. It's misleading and can cause confusion, as it did here). So the gss nt hostbased service name you want is going to be HTTP@poke.int.example.org, which is quite similar to the principal HTTP/poke.int.example.org@INT.EXAMPLE.ORG.

If you were using MIT kerberos, I"d suggest you try running the code with the environment variable KRB5_TRACE set to /dev/stderr -- but I don't know how to get similar debugging output from heimdal.

The current project-check [cascade][1] is:

1. `DATASTORE_DATASET` environment variable, set by `gcd` tool (only [sometimes][2])
1. `GCLOUD_PROJECT` environment variable
1. App Engine App ID
1. GCE project ID

I'm suggesting you inject two more checks in there (not sure about the order yet):

- Check for a project ID in a JSON svc. acct. keyfile specified by `GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS`
- Check in the `${HOME}/.config/gcloud/configurations/config_default` file

----

For reference the [cascade][3] for credentials [is][4]:

1. GAE application ID service
1. filename via `GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS`
1. the "well-known" file set by the `gcloud` CLI (`${HOME}/.config/gcloud/application_default_credentials.json`)
1. GCE instance creds

On the "del ts[a:b]", I agree it is rather ambiguous ... It is indeed the equivalent of remove_measurements_from_interval (or maybe better remove_points_in_interval) as you describe. It does not set the default value, as the default value is better seen (see previous post) as the "original value" on domain.lower. On the include/exclude, I would rather assume all operations are on a right-continuous curve and so we operate on [a,b) (to keep a consistent interpretation of bounds across traces). 
The effect of a "del ts[a:b]" would be to change the value on [b, ...) to the value of ts[a-epsilon] which is quite consistent with the simple "del ts[a]" that will set the value on [a,...) to the value just before a ts[a-epsilon]
