could you expand a little on the use case(s) here?

in general, the idea of editable deps + pex seems to be at odds with the pex core philosophy of 'hermetically sealed artifacts', which aims to avoid side effects from environment to environment. editable deps appear to be designed to solve a problem with traditional python installations/venvs where deps are "installed" into the environment vs composed into a standalone executable. in the pex/pants world, you'd typically just iterate by building and running another pex - tho arguably it's not quite as fast of a development cycle as you might achieve with something like editable deps.

it's also worth noting that pex files can run uncompressed (`unzip -l the.pex && python2.7 .`), in which case their copy of contents are editable (including the sources of the transitive dependency set). this allows for quick debugging/iteration in more complex multi-package scenarios than even editable deps might.

John,
I was able to create bullet and numbered lists using this code:

```python
from docx import Document
from docx.shared import Inches
from docx.oxml import OxmlElement
from docx.oxml.ns import qn

def create_list(paragraph, list_type):
    p = paragraph._p #access to xml paragraph element
    pPr = p.get_or_add_pPr() #access paragraph properties
    numPr = OxmlElement('w:numPr') #create number properties element
    numId = OxmlElement('w:numId') #create numId element - sets bullet type
    numId.set(qn('w:val'), list_type) #set list type/indentation
    numPr.append(numId) #add bullet type to number properties list
    pPr.append(numPr) #add number properties to paragraph

ordered = "5"
unordered = "1"

document = Document()

paragraph = document.add_paragraph("Hello", "List Paragraph")
create_list(paragraph, unordered)

paragraph = document.add_paragraph("Hello Again", "List Paragraph")
create_list(paragraph, unordered)

paragraph = document.add_paragraph("Goodbye", "List Paragraph")
create_list(paragraph, unordered)

paragraph = document.add_paragraph("Hello", "List Paragraph")
create_list(paragraph, ordered)

paragraph = document.add_paragraph("Hello Again", "List Paragraph")
create_list(paragraph, ordered)

paragraph = document.add_paragraph("Goodbye", "List Paragraph")
create_list(paragraph, ordered)

document.save("bullet list demo.docx")
```

The list style is called "List Paragraph" and the type of bullet is set by adding the appropriate XML elements.  python-docx apparently creates a default "numbering" XML document which governs the styles and indentation of list items; the list_type I put into numId is a reference to that document.

YMMV, I don't know that this is the best way to do this, but it works...

The mime type of the file indicates that it is a Google Docs file. These files require you to specify the file type you want to download it as. For example: Download a Google Drive Document as Microsoft Word file. 
In order to download all files from GDrive (which seems to be what you are doing) I would suggest you define a dict which maps the Google Drive file type to its desired download mime type.

In your code prior to the loop, define a dict similar to this:
```
mimetypes = {
    # Drive Document files as MS Word files.
    'application/vnd.google-apps.document': 'application/vnd.openxmlformats-officedocument.wordprocessingml.document',

    # Drive Sheets files as MS Excel files.
    'application/vnd.google-apps.spreadsheet': 'application/vnd.openxmlformats-officedocument.spreadsheetml.sheet'

    # etc.
}
```
You can find a list of the mime types Google Drive uses [here](https://developers.google.com/drive/v2/web/mime-types), and a list of mime types you can convert these to [here](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/4212861/what-is-a-correct-mime-type-for-docx-pptx-etc/4212908#4212908).

You can then adapt the loop to handle these mimetypes:

```
for file1 in file_list:
    download_mimetype = ''
    if file1['mimeType'] in mimetypes:
        download_mimetype = mimetypes[file1['mimeType']]

    file1.GetContentFile(file1['title'], mimetype=download_mimetype)
```
**Note**: You don't need to create a new file object to download the file. Further, the maximum file size you can download with PyDrive is currently limited by the size of your memory.

Have a look at [this PyDrive issue](https://github.com/googledrive/PyDrive/issues/64) for an example of requesting a file with a specific mimetype, and if `mimetype=mimetype` seems confusing have a look at the [Python docs on Keyword Arguments](https://docs.python.org/2/tutorial/controlflow.html#keyword-arguments).

Hope this helps!

Hi, ExaBGP execute your program into a thread and read the std.out, but ExaBGP drop all permission before, so it's the user nobody who execute your program. So you can run sudo command for example to take some user permission (no recommended). For you, the best way to test your program is with the command : `` sudo -u nobody `` It's only a permission problem in your case, not a ExaBGP issue. Azlux

There is another reason, besides hinting, why one would want to decompose transformed components, which is the issue reported in https://github.com/googlei18n/fontmake/issues/22, namely the fact that different rasterizers behave differently as regards composite glyphs and transforms that reverse the winding direction, which manifest itself when components overlap with each other.

The consesus over there seems to be to always decompose such composite glyphs, to prevent things like that.

I think what we should do is to always decompose components when they (1) intersect other contours/components, and (2) have a reversing transform.

Support for the custom parameters that Georg mentioned could also be added to fontmake/glyphsLib/ufo2ft, but if ttfautohint does the right thing (i.e. only hint composites if any component has a non-translation transform, like Adam and Behdad suggested) then we wouldn't need an extra custom parameter that optionally decomposes them.

@nkeim  Thanks for you feedback.

> Functions like batch() already have so many kwargs that we need to be careful about adding more.

Why not collect all arguments that are passed through to `locate` in an argument `locate_kws` or  as `**kwargs` (not as explicit but wouldn't break the API)? I mean `batch` is nothing but a convenient wrapper for `locate` anyway and this would even follow the DRY principle concerning the docstring of `batch` which repeats the one from `locate`.

> Could the job of report_hook be done via calls to logging? 

Yeah it could but would be the inferior solution in my opinion. While wrapping trackpy for a command line tool I was a bit annoyed that I couldn't access the progress of `batch` (a really long running function) in a non-hacky way. Using the logging module wasn't enough, because I wanted to control the output in a more sophisticated way, in this case a progress bar. Besides the logging approach in trackpy only allows to configure logging for the whole library (at least that's what I think; I honestly find the logging module a little complex and difficult to grasp). So the best solution I could think of was to wrap `locate` in my own version of `batch` with a `report_hook`. Then I added threading and finally arrived on this PR. 

I think, especially for long running functions, there should be a way to overwrite the default behavior in a well defined way. With this approach the user is free to suppress all logging, if unwanted, but can still access the progress of long running functions if needed. 

Another advantage in this case might be, that `report_hook` could be used (abused?) for more complex tasks right after feature detection. But I can't really think of any right now. :smile:

> Or, is there a more general way we could build that into trackpy? (See e.g. the quiet() and handle_logging() functions in trackpy.utils)

I didn't even know those existed. It might be worthwhile to figure out whether these or `report_hook` enable the best solution. And I agree, library wide consistency is the more important point.

From #1878

> If I do `conda install -f jupyter` then I want to force-install all the dependency packages specified the jupyter meta-package.

@ilanschnell, @ijstokes: I know this has come up many times, that somehow `conda install --force` should pull all dependencies, too. But when I fully think through the ramifications of this I find I simply can't get to a satisfactory conclusion.

For instance, `jupyter` depends on `python`. Which Python do I choose? Do I query the environment to see what version it's using? Or what if a package is built for multiple NumPy versions; should I query the environment to figure out which version of NumPy I should prefer? Either way I'm not _really_ ignoring the environment, which goes against the `--force` flag. I should be picking Python 3.5 and NumPy 1.11 every time unless an alternative is added right there on the command line.

But let's set that aside, and say the rule is: pretend the environment is empty. What if the packages I ask for are _still_ internally inconsistent? Should conda cough up an `Unsatisfiable` exception? But isn't `--force` basically supposed to always work? 

Perhaps the rule is: if it's satisfiable, make it so; but if not, just do the "best you can" in some sense. What exactly is that sense? Perhaps it should just pick the latest versions of the requested packages, and the latest versions of their dependencies, and so on. There will be a lot of tiebreaking to do, I'm guessing.

I think the desire for `--force-but-with-dependencies-too` simply doesn't have a well-defined specification. If someone can spec out all of these corner cases then we could put something like it on the roadmap. But since `--force` has behaved this way for over three years, I think we need a new name. Maybe `--force` `--ignore-installed`.

IMHO, we should fix the source of the problem rather than patching over it ..

Arguably `conda` should be giving us _NEW_PART in a shell-appropriate form, which would involve using `cygpath -u` internally, something like: https://github.com/conda/conda-build/commit/e1e905#diff-c4cfa32f287679b20f86b99df93b3188R460, this would then work for both `MSYS2` and `Cygwin` and also it would respect `/etc/fstab`.
An alternative is if we can come up with an easy way on the conda-build side to do the right thing, but it would have to work with conda 4.3 and 4.4...
We no longer link Python dynamically to libpython.so (but we still provide this). Instead our Python interpreter is statically linked to libpython.a. This is done for performance reasons.

> First, thanks for the awesome, detailed report. I wish every issue filed was this descriptive :)

Thanks, it's the least I can do. 😁 

> Question 1: What are the details of your file system/mount for your W:\ drive? Local? Network? NTFS? Other?

That is a normal, local partition. In almost all of our slaves that's a separate hard-drive than the one where the system is installed.

A detail that perhaps it wasn't very clear is that we checked the permissions of the `.trash` folder and the files on it and they were fine. We can delete the files normally when logging in with the same user which runs our CI job once we kill the python/conda process.

> Are you doing this with the same automated script that is calling conda update or conda install? 
> What is the actual command you're using?

Yes, we are doing that in two scripts at the moment:

1. A powershell script which is run daily to provision the workers (ensure that conda is installed, update some choco packages, cleanup some workspaces, etc). That script is now running this command right before attempting to execute `conda install`:

  ```ps
  $cir = conda info --root
  $trash_dir = "$($cir)\pkgs\.trash"
  if (Test-Path $trash_dir){
      Remove-Item -Recurse -Force $trash_dir
  }
  conda --debug update --all --yes --quiet 
  ```

2. A "setup worker" BAT file which executed at the start of each job, and calls `conda env update` to create a conda-environment:

  ```bat
  for /F %%i in ('conda info --root') do set CONDA_ROOT=%%i
  set CONDA_TRASH=%CONDA_ROOT%\pkgs\.trash
  if exist %CONDA_TRASH% (
      echo Removing %CONDA_TRASH%
      rd /s /q %CONDA_TRASH%
  )
  @REM update conda and conda-env to the versions specified on this branch
  call conda install -n root --file "%WORKSPACE%\eden\conda_version.txt" || goto ERROR
  ```

The `conda install/update` calls above are the points where we were observing that conda was getting stuck.

We have a separate `W:` drive because we can easily quick format it and run the provisioning script to "wipe" a slave. The `W:` drive contains Miniconda, all packages and environments, as well as the jobs' workspaces.

@minrk: @jjhelmus and I had a good internal discussion about my proposal above, and in hindsight we should have summarized our findings there or here. In short, we concluded that 1) it would cause some problems to automatically construct build groups according to the build string convention; and 2) changes coming to `conda-build` would render this approach irrelevant _anyway_, because build strings are going to start being built according to a different convetion.

That said, it is my view that `pyzmq` should _not_ be doing what it is doing---specifically, using build  _numbers_ to differentiate between packages that are no different except in their dependencies. This is something that _should_ be resolved using build _string_ differences instead, and the two should have identical build numbers. So for instance, the packages should be something like this:

`pyzmq-16.0.2-py36zmq41_0`
`pyzmq-16.0.2-py36zmq42_0`

I understand that this is difficult to accomplish without some serious Jinja wizardry, but I'm genuinely concerned that there are material consequences to the solver caused by this pinning practice.

Speed and spec format.

Conda build allows specs according to https://conda.io/docs/spec.html#package-match-specifications

The command line interface and the newer conda API, which is just a tiny bit lower level than the CLI, do not support those match specs.  Part of the reason is that characters like > and < get misinterpreted by the terminal. Even if you put them on quotes, though, the CLI still doesn't support them.

Speed is also drastically slower, because conda build is able to control caching more intelligently.

I'm surprised that the hints are different, but it probably comes down to the input specs being different. I had to alter them in order to make them valid CLI input. Ultimately conda's solver is being called similarly in both conda build and conda.

We're working on a conda API that will unify behavior while maintaining the speed advantages.

Another idea: completely ditch this "type" idea and focus on https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/988, and separating different types into different channels. 

**Advantages:**

- Channels are already a well developed feature of Binstar, and are managed by conda well.

- Doesn't change the package name spec at all. The only part of the package spec that changes is the dependency spec.

**Disadvantages:**

- Not so easy, at least with current tooling, to split out packages into different channels.

- One of the original concerns from #988 comes to the forefront here, which is that you end up requiring packages to come from some "official" channel only. Hence, we can't *really* say that the "r" Binstar channel is the same as an "r" type, because only official maintainers of the "r" Binstar channel can upload packages to that channel.  This would also encourage people to depend on the packages from that specific channel rather than on the package alone when they otherwise wouldn't, which I think is a bad thing.

@mcg1969, for reference, we should be aware of the following sections of two PEPs.  I just randomly stumbled onto these via reading source code for the `pkginfo` package.  I wasn't aware of them before, and it's another good reference.

## From [PEP-314](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0314/)

    Provides (multiple use)

      Each entry contains a string describing a package or module that
      will be provided by this package once it is installed.  These
      strings should match the ones used in Requirements fields.  A
      version declaration may be supplied (without a comparison
      operator); the package's version number will be implied if none
      is specified.

      Example: 

          Provides: xml
          Provides: xml.utils
          Provides: xml.utils.iso8601
          Provides: xml.dom
          Provides: xmltools (1.3)

    Obsoletes (multiple use)

      Each entry contains a string describing a package or module
      that this package renders obsolete, meaning that the two packages
      should not be installed at the same time.  Version declarations
      can be supplied.  

      The most common use of this field will be in case a package name
      changes, e.g. Gorgon 2.3 gets subsumed into Torqued Python 1.0.
      When you install Torqued Python, the Gorgon package should be
      removed.
      
      Example:

          Obsoletes: Gorgon

## From [PEP-345](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0345/#requires-dist-multiple-use)

### Requires-Dist (multiple use)

Each entry contains a string naming some other distutils project required by this distribution.

The format of a requirement string is identical to that of a distutils project name (e.g., as found in the Name: field. optionally followed by a version declaration within parentheses.

The distutils project names should correspond to names as found on the Python Package Index [4] .

Version declarations must follow the rules described in Version Specifiers

Examples:

    Requires-Dist: pkginfo
    Requires-Dist: PasteDeploy
    Requires-Dist: zope.interface (>3.5.0)


### Provides-Dist (multiple use)

Each entry contains a string naming a Distutils project which is contained within this distribution. This field must include the project identified in the Name field, followed by the version : Name (Version).

A distribution may provide additional names, e.g. to indicate that multiple projects have been bundled together. For instance, source distributions of the ZODB project have historically included the transaction project, which is now available as a separate distribution. Installing such a source distribution satisfies requirements for both ZODB and transaction .

A distribution may also provide a "virtual" project name, which does not correspond to any separately-distributed project: such a name might be used to indicate an abstract capability which could be supplied by one of multiple projects. E.g., multiple projects might supply RDBMS bindings for use by a given ORM: each project might declare that it provides ORM-bindings , allowing other projects to depend only on having at most one of them installed.

A version declaration may be supplied and must follow the rules described in Version Specifiers . The distribution's version number will be implied if none is specified.

Examples:

    Provides-Dist: OtherProject
    Provides-Dist: AnotherProject (3.4)
    Provides-Dist: virtual_package


### Obsoletes-Dist (multiple use)
Each entry contains a string describing a distutils project's distribution which this distribution renders obsolete, meaning that the two projects should not be installed at the same time.

Version declarations can be supplied. Version numbers must be in the format specified in Version Specifiers .

The most common use of this field will be in case a project name changes, e.g. Gorgon 2.3 gets subsumed into Torqued Python 1.0. When you install Torqued Python, the Gorgon distribution should be removed.

Examples:

Obsoletes-Dist: Gorgon
Obsoletes-Dist: OtherProject (<3.0)


### Requires-Python
This field specifies the Python version(s) that the distribution is guaranteed to be compatible with.

Version numbers must be in the format specified in Version Specifiers .

Examples:

    Requires-Python: 2.5
    Requires-Python: >2.1
    Requires-Python: >=2.3.4
    Requires-Python: >=2.5,<2.7


### Requires-External (multiple use)

Each entry contains a string describing some dependency in the system that the distribution is to be used. This field is intended to serve as a hint to downstream project maintainers, and has no semantics which are meaningful to the distutils distribution.

The format of a requirement string is a name of an external dependency, optionally followed by a version declaration within parentheses.

Because they refer to non-Python software releases, version numbers for this field are not required to conform to the format specified in PEP 440 : they should correspond to the version scheme used by the external dependency.

Notice that there's is no particular rule on the strings to be used.

Examples:

    Requires-External: C
    Requires-External: libpng (>=1.5)

That's an interesting point - are Python project "releases" on Github typically identical to what's on PyPI?

My understanding of a "Github release" is that it's basically just a tar.gz containing the current state of the Github repo as of the time we tagged a release.  It includes things like our ``.gitignore`` and ``.travis.yaml`` and such.  That's different than what a ``python setup.py sdist`` will generate, which is what ends up on PyPI and which shouldn't really include those things.

I'd therefore assumed until now that a "Github release" and a "PyPI release" were fundamentally different things and that it's okay for them to have different contents so long as the actual Python source files which they include are identical.  I'm happy to be corrected if that's not the accepted standard though.

*Floating point* values have very different bits even for close values, so shuffle is of little help. In .NET world I am converting floats to decimals (no loss of precision) and taking diff (no loss of precision for decimals) - then shuffle shines on the diff values. Taking diff directly on floats loses precision and will be useless since the diff values are also floating point and suffer from different bits unless the diff is exactly the same (very rare cases). For real data (e.g. stock prices) I have quite high compression with decimals compared to floats, yet the speed is much slower but still faster than IO - I use Blosc for disk storage and network, not in-memory data transfer. Another point is that for certain data, e.g. financial, you have to store data as decimal even if you use it in analytical calculations as floats - to avoid issues line `0.9-0.6-0.3 <> 0.0`, so for me decimal was very good solution.

Well, this seems to explain it.  You have 4 digit "dates" in your _kibana-int-foo_:

```
kibana-int-ccosvms0053
```

You're doing a date regex on `%Y`, which is a 4 digit number.

```
2015-08-28 16:14:02,656 DEBUG     curator.cli.index_selection                indices:94   Filter: {'pattern': '(?P<date>\\d{4})', 'value': 365, 'groupname': 'date', 'time_unit': 'days', 'timestring': u'%Y', 'method': 'older_than'}
```

As you can see, the filter is only looking for `\d{4)`, or 4 digits.  You don't have `kibana-int-foo`, but rather `kibana-int-foo\d{4}`, _which is a match_, where `0053` is a much older year than 2015 :smile: 

You can still use the year number as a pattern.   I would simply add `--exclude '^kibana'` as a means to prevent those from being otherwise matched. (It may need to be a full regex, like `'^kibana-int-.*$'`).

@serg666 

So in your example at the bottom of your issue, this is ok because Elasticsearch will attempt to index the data `"foo"` as a type `text`. Then in the second index request you're sending an integer and Elasticsearch will just do a type conversion for you. 

In the issue you're having what's happening is the first value that gets index is of type `long` and Elasticearch will create the field with the `long` type. Then the when it sees a value of type `text` it can't convert a `somestring` to an interger. 

going from `123` to `"123"` is ok. 
Going from `"foobar"` to `foobar` is not ok. 

So in order to fix what you have to do is create a [mapping](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/current/mapping.html) for your index at index creation time or create a [template](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/current/indices-templates.html) for which any index created matching the `index_pattern` will use a mapping that you defined in the template. And in your mapping you'll have to define the field type as `text` or `keyword` so that your data can get index appropriately.
Thanks!

> Out of curiosity, which specific line fixes the "windows browse issue" ? :)

Switching from `NamedTemporaryFile` to the less convenient `mkstemp`. On Windows only, a `NamedTemporaryFile` cannot be opened more than once, which we do indirectly here by passing it as an argument to `kubectl`. But `mkstemp` works and is considered secure.

@iandees, I’d love your opinion on whether this is a good idea.

Sure! So to be clear this is not to validate that the file as stored on S3 is corrupt in some way, but simply to ensure that the version that someone downloads is identical to the one on S3. Like I said there are of course many ways during downloading and storing the data that errors can crop up. In particular, S3 in my experience is very happy to return partial content responses, especially if there happens to be a significant amount of traffic at that moment. Its true you can use the content-length to check for this, but its still annoying.

Assuming no one is [being deliberately evil](http://www.mscs.dal.ca/~selinger/md5collision/), even an md5 hash has an overwhelming probability of being unique so files with matching checksums are likely to be the same. 

The procedure I want to implement for downloading all the files for pelias is something like this.

0.) A download directory already exists, and already contains zip files from previous runs which may be a week or more old.
1.) Check each entry in the `state.txt` file: if a file with that filename and hash exist, continue to the next. Otherwise, remove any file with that name and redownload. Check the newly downloaded file against the hash again, to verify it's correct. Possibly, repeat this some bounded number of times if the file continues to be corrupt.
2.) Extract all the zip files to a working directory and import them into pelias.

Even with content lengths and last modified headers, its possible for data errors to sneak through. With a hash, its possible to be confident that the file downloaded is not corrupt without checking anything else!

The following list, the proposed signals that would be implemented taking into account your comments. All of them have at least the same first two arguments, the *client_session* and *request* instance objects related to the specific request that is being traced. Later other divergent arguments can be considered taking into account each specific event.

* `on_request_start(client_session, request)`
* `on_dns_start(client_session, request)`
* `on_dns_end(client_session, request)`
* `on_request_queued(client_session, request)`
* `on_request_unqueued(client_session, request)`
* `on_createconn_start(client_session, request)`
* `on_createconn_end(client_session, request)`
* `on_connection_adquired(client_session, request)`
* `on_connection_released(client_session, request)`
* `on_request_headers_sent(client_session, request)`
* `on_request_body_sent(client_session, request)`
* `on_request_headers_received(client_session, request)`
* `on_request_content_received(client_session, request)`
* `on_request_end(client_session, request)`

The use case is represented by the following snippet:

```python
async def request_start(client_session, request):
    print("Request {} started".format(request))

async def request_end(client_session, request):
    print("Request {} finished".format(request))

request_tracing = RequestTracing()
request_traccing.on_request_start.add(request_start)
request_traccing.on_request_end.add(request_end)
session = ClientSession(request_tracing=request_tracing)
```

**Under the hood**

Before PEP 550 [1] is not available, I will go for an ad-hoc implementation that stores at task level
the object that would allow sending a signal by any piece of code involved to make a request placed at almost any part of the code. It will give us the following pros:

- Request tracing is explicitly activated at instantiation time using the `ClientSession` object. 
- Underlying objects such as `BaseConnector` u others do not pollute their signatures, introspecting at real time if the request tracing is enabled at some point.
- Each trace, a unique code path executed at the moment making a request, is accompanied with the `ClientSession` and the `Request` instance.

The following snippet shows how the signal related to the `on_request_queued` and `on_request_unqueued` operation might be implemented inside of the `BaseConnector` code:

```python
class BaseConnector:
    .....

    @asyncio.coroutine
    def connect(self, req):
        ....

        # Wait if there are no available connections.
        if available <= 0:
            fut = helpers.create_future(self._loop)

            # This connection will now count towards the limit.
            waiters = self._waiters[key]
            waiters.append(fut)

            if RequestTracing.trace():
                yield from RequestTracing.trace().on_request_queued.send()

            yield from fut

            if RequestTracing.trace():
                yield from RequestTracing.trace().on_request_unqueued.send()

            waiters.remove(fut)

        ....
```

The `trace` method returns an object that was created and stored at the task level pointing to
the `ClientSession` and the `Request` object. The following code might show how this object/trace is created when the `ClientSession._request` method is called:

```python

class ClientSession

    @asyncio.coroutine
    def _request(....):
        ....
        req = self._request_class(..........)
        if request_tracing:
            trace = request_tracing.new_trace(self, req)
            yield from trace.on_requests_start.send()
        ...
```

At the moment that `request_tracing.new_trace` is executed a new trace at task level is stored bound to the `ClientSession` and the `Request` instance, making it available for the underlying objects.

[1] https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0550/

> How to check that a new `botocore` release is compatible with the current master of `aiobotocore` allowing to release automatically a new `aiobotocore` paired with the `botocore` one?

My concern about that is. Despite, we will be able to inject the perfect layer to make botocore async we will be still forced to check the whole functionality. And even in that case, the integration/acceptance/functional tests that are provided by botocore must be modified to make them asynchronous. And this is still a huge and time-consuming work, having also the disadvantage to keep them synchronized with the original ones. And this is for me the same problem that you raised.

Here I can see only three solutions:

1) Rewrite the test suite to make it compatible with the asynchronous world. The disadvantages are clear enough.
2) Introduce a thin layer between the test suite and `aiobotocore` as an extra step for the release to check the test suite to check it with the asynchronous client. No fucking idea how to do it right now, but will reduce a lot the effort needed.
3) The rationale of run the test suite is invalid.

No problem; the package [pweave](http://mpastell.com/pweave/) is currently
my go to package for generating reports with python - though it's a long
way off R's [KnitR](http://yihui.name/knitr/) in terms of functionality;
have a look at the pweave examples [here](
http://mpastell.com/pweave/examples/) and KnitR examples [here](
https://github.com/yihui/knitr-examples).

The philosophy is admittedly slightly different from a notebook - your goal
from the start is to write code chronologically such that you 'Restart &
Run All' at the end to generate your document. However, enabling this
functionality could be quite simple: adding '[chunk](
http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com/authoring_rcodechunks.html) [options](
http://mpastell.com/pweave/chunks.html)' to all code cells should allow
jupyter notebooks to be used for this purpose without any other changes.

From my perspective, the reason why this style of working is important is
twofold:

* Make creating reports easier
    - no faffing with latex (though you can use latex within the document
when you want - already implemented in jupyter markdown cells)
    - write the report as you code
    - allow inline references to the data and results e.g. instead of
writing 'The mean of the column was 2.6' you would write 'The mean of the
column was `np.mean(some_array)`', such that, if and when your data
changes, you simply regenerate your report, safe in the knowledge the text
is still correct
    - 1000s of researchers worldwide would benefit from the time saved
* Make reports reproducible
    - You know exactly what code you executed to create all the figures,
tables, and text
    - Reproducibility in academic research is currently [pretty poor]() -
creating tools to make this easier will have widespread benefits to the
academic community

I think a good first step would be a markdown cell toolbar that controls
simple cell options like hiding (like the 'edit metadata' cell toolbar but
higher level). Most of the back end work appears to already be done - I
detail a solution [here](
http://stackoverflow.com/questions/31517194/how-to-hide-one-specific-cell-input-or-output-in-ipython-notebook/38040507#38040507)
that uses an nbextension - but an easy user interface that doesn't require
faffing with the cell metadata would be useful such that the user can see
at a glance what to expect the generated report to look like.

A more difficult next step, I imagine, would be thinking about how to
implement caching and whether that idea is compatible with the notebook
philosophy.

I'm keen to contribute if I can so do let me know if I can help in some way.

I've been sketching a new exception hierarchy that looks like this:

* BaseError
  * FieldError
    * ConversionError
    * ValidationError
    * StopValidationError
  * CompoundError
    * DataError

All `FieldError` subclasses represent field-specific errors arising from the field's own validators, while errors bubbling up from substructures within compound fields are represented by a `CompoundError`.

Therefore, `FieldError.messages` is now guaranteed to be a flat list (as is currently the case for errors on basic types).

`CompoundError.messages`, on the other hand, is always guaranteed to be a dictionary, keyed by the offending item's identifier. For a `ListType`, this identifier is the item's index.

`DataError` is a `CompoundError` representing the result of validating an entire model. It's basically identical to `ModelConversionError` and `ModelValidationError`, both of which will be deprecated.

Error messages are no longer just strings but `ErrorMessage` objects containing three attributes:
  * type (conversion or validation error)
  * summary (the human-readable message)
  * info (an arbitrary value; e.g., an error code or some other response from the validator)

#### Example

```python
class M(Model):
    intfield = IntType(max_value=3)
    listfield = ListType(IntType(max_value=3))
    modelfield = ModelType('M')

inputdict = {
    'intfield': 1,
    'modelfield': {
        'intfield': 2,
        'modelfield': {

These are two separate issues. @Etragas's change ensures that the outgrads list associated with a node are garbage collected after they're finished being used. The issue that @alexbw is raising is that it's expensive to accumulate the outgrads in a list to begin with. That is, @Etragas saves memory across multiple nodes' outgrads lists, whereas @alexbw is concerned about memory usage for a single node's outgrad list.

I believe @dougalm is implementing a different accumulation strategy for the outgrads that will primarily address the issue @alexbw raised, though the change @Etragas suggested (explicitly deleting the entry from the outgrads dict) should also be included, since we don't need to keep those around.

> How about this for this sprint?
>
>  *  Subdomains can only be created by their domain names.
>  *  Any domain name can broadcast a subdomain zone file update, but not an address change.

That sounds good to me.

Regarding your earlier comments:

> Doesn't this make subdomain lookups just as expensive as they were when done client-side? i.e. each lookup still has to process a subdomain's history?

Why? We could support a query that allows the client to find out whether or not a given domain has any missing zonefiles earlier than some transaction. This query is cheap for the indexer, and cheap for the client.

>Also, if any zone file is missing, then wouldn't this make warnings into false positives in many cases?

Not sure I understand this -- in this case, wouldn't it be _worse_ if blockstackd just didn't process any subdomain registrations?

In general, I think it's possible that `blockstackd` refusing to process subdomain updates if a zonefile is missing can work, but it's really important that if that's the case, we have a plan for dealing with missing zonefiles due to errors or bugs (after all, there are many missing zonefiles currently) before shipping that behavior.

The variable that is being unused should have a meaningful name. As for your example I think it is silly but, again, all you have to do is this:

(var_a, var_b) = some_func()
_ = var_b
```

We definitely do not want to have the check for being unused suppressed for every var that begins with an underscore.

It's probably worth describing in more detail what this patch is doing:

1. Drop django-haystack completely.
2. Create a new search backend that is powered by Elasticsearch. It does not support Solr or any other search engine (but you could write your own that exposes the same basic API). Using a single search engine allows us to use its features (e.g., faceting and aggregations) to the fullest extent, rather than lowest-common-denominator which is what we ended up with when using Haystack.
3. Provide support for indexing of *any* document, not just products.
4. For the catalogue, provide a number of helpful utilities and classes, including:
     a) Built-in support for indexing and faceting of attributes - any attribute for which a facet is defined in `OSCAR_SEARCH['FACETS']` will be automatically indexed and facets generated during search.
     b) Dynamic price range faceting
     c) Category filtering
     d) Auto-suggest querying, filterable by category
     e) Top and related products querying, filterable by category

The filtering and faceting capability that this introduces is significantly more powerful and flexible than what is currently possible with Haystack, and is the main motivation for this change.

This seems reasonable. Feel free to make a pull request. One thing I'd be a bit worried about is cross-platform compatibility here. You can probably pull it from the filename, but on *nix systems, I'm getting:

```python
    >>> from dateutil import tz
    >>> tzla = tz.gettz('America/Los_Angeles')
    >>> print(tzla._filename)
    '/usr/share/zoneinfo/America/Los_Angeles'
    >>> tzmet = tz.gettz('MET')
    >>> print(tzmet._filename)
    '/usr/share/zoneinfo/MET'
```

Whereas on Windows I'm getting:


```python
    >>> from dateutil import tz
    >>> tzla = tz.gettz('America/Los_Angeles')
    >>> print(tzla._filename)
    'America/Los_Angeles'
    >>> tzmet = tz.gettz('MET')
    >>> print(tzmet._filename)
    'MET'
```

I'm guessing this is because on the *nix systems it's using the system zoneinfo file, and on Windows it's using the one provided by dateutil. If you decide not to make a PR, I can dig into this later.

See #258 for the explanation of the current behavior. It did change and I didn't realize so many people were counting on the old (undocumented) behavior, but a broader change to how the parser handles things is needed.

See also #402 for the same issue, and #214 for a similar issue.

By the way, on the subject of consistency, I don't think it really matters if the behavior is consistent between python 2 and 3 *too* much, because the existence of a default comparison operator is one of the well-known differences between Python 2 and Python 3. If we *did* want it to be consistent (and I'm not convinced of the value of this), the best way is probably to just do:

```python
    def __cmp__(self, other):
        raise TypeError('Comparison is not supported between types {} and {}'.format(type(self), type(other)))
```

You're extending a `+Trigger` with those `^Continue` commands, so **an** error is expected. 😀 

The code is equivalent to (with the default concat mode):

```
+ ok1) test2) test
```

What's not expected, though, is that the error came from the Python regexp module and not [my own code](https://github.com/aichaos/rivescript-python/blob/ac9d56b19e550ba68e857d4aedb96c654da91cfc/rivescript/parser.py#L557-L574). In the RiveScript parser, I syntax check triggers by counting opening and closing brackets and check for obvious mismatches (it's still possible to write a trigger that has all the brackets match up but that is still an invalid regexp, so my bracket counting code is just a quick sanity check to prevent *obvious* errors).

The syntax check currently happens *before* `^Continue` commands are joined together, so it should be moved to after that. Then you'd get a parse-time error from RiveScript rather than a reply-time error from Python. :wink:

Made some investigation:
1. scala example works with Spark 2.3
2. python example will work if any of the hierarchical DataFrame is cached, with Spark 2.3
3. python pipeline example without images works with 2.3.

```

from bigdl.nn.criterion import *
from bigdl.nn.layer import *
from bigdl.optim.optimizer import *
from numpy.testing import assert_allclose
from pyspark.ml import Pipeline
from pyspark.ml.feature import MinMaxScaler
from pyspark.sql.types import *

from zoo.common.nncontext import *
from zoo.pipeline.nnframes import *
from zoo.feature.common import *
from zoo.feature.image import *


from pyspark.mllib.linalg import Vectors
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, udf
from pyspark.sql.types import DoubleType, StringType

if __name__ == "__main__":

    sparkConf = create_spark_conf().setMaster("local[1]").setAppName("testNNClassifer")
    sc = init_nncontext(sparkConf)
    sqlContext = SQLContext(sc)

    df = sqlContext.createDataFrame(
        [(Vectors.dense([2.0, 1.0]), 1.0),
         (Vectors.dense([1.0, 2.0]), 2.0),
         (Vectors.dense([2.0, 1.0]), 1.0),
         (Vectors.dense([1.0, 2.0]), 2.0),
         ], ["features", "l"])

    getLabel = udf(lambda d: d, DoubleType())
    labelDF = df.withColumn("label", getLabel(col('l')))

    model1 = Sequential().add(Linear(2, 2))
    first = NNModel(model1) \
        .setFeaturesCol("features") \
        .setPredictionCol("first")

    model2 = Sequential().add(Linear(2, 2))
    criterion = ClassNLLCriterion()
    classifier = NNClassifier(model2, criterion) \
        .setBatchSize(4) \
        .setLearningRate(0.01).setMaxEpoch(1).setFeaturesCol("first")

    pipeline = Pipeline(stages=[first, classifier])

    pipelineModel = pipeline.fit(labelDF)

    res = pipelineModel.transform(labelDF)
```

Yes, sorry, I meant using the [local version identifier](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0440/#local-version-identifiers) to include the number of commits and commit SHA1 for versions in between releases, as in the `0.11+2.g1076c97.dirty` style.

> pip freeze should help with writing the requirements file. It allows you to collect all requirements at the time you write the experiment in one file.

Hmm, given that I should also record the version of Theano, probably that's the cleanest way. However, if I rely on Theano not changing in incompatible ways and `lasagne.__version__` was including the commit SHA1, I could just record that along with the model files, which would be a little more convenient than invoking and parsing `subprocess.check_output(['pip', 'freeze'])` or figuring out the equivalent sequence of `pip` function calls.

> And then in setup.py follow pep440, where proper release version numbers are e.g. '1.0' and development versions (i.e. our versions in Git) have e.g. '1.0.dev' (which would be the development version that comes before 1.0, according to the PEP).

So we should change that in our current `setup.py` (it currently misses the dot).

> The end result wouldn't be much different to what you're suggesting, it's just more in line with what tools like pip would expect.

:+1:

> That's correct. Though it also means this user ignored the installation instructions (which seems to happen frequently enough).

The world isn't perfect. Even with updated documentation, people will use whatever version they have installed, and report bugs. We should provide an easy way for them to let us know which version they have installed. `pip freeze | grep Lasagne` would work, but place some additional burden on us (such as running `git describe` to make sense of the SHA1 commit id). I still don't think `0.11+2.g1076c97.dirty` would be superfluous, but I agree that we shouldn't include a thousand code lines for that functionality.


So... until we can find a simpler "versioneer" variant ([the one from params](https://github.com/ioam/param/blob/master/param/version.py) seems to miss some features) that lowers the barrier, do you want to update the version in `setup.py` to `0.1.dev` and add a `__version__ = ` snippet to `__init__.py`? We can then update #262 to link to the latest master whenever `__version__` contains `dev`, and to a specific version otherwise. (We cannot link to the correct commit for development versions with this versioning scheme.) That will at least give us *something* for the first release.

> Do you have any usescases that will not be supported when `TensorVariable` is removed?

Note that `TensorVariable` will not actually be removed, just the special-case handling in the recurrent layers. Due to #11, any layer parameter can be a `TensorVariable` already. The difference will be that after removing the special case, `hid_init` being a `TensorVariable` would be treated the same as `hid_init` being a `SharedVariable` or `ndarray` or `callable`, i.e., it will be a (potentially learnable) parameter replicated over the batch.

If you remove the special case for `TensorVariable`, a `TensorSharedVariable` will work fine, but it will also be suspect to replication over the batch.

Instead of #543, you should remove the special case for `TensorVariable` and add a parameter that controls whether `hid_init` is to be replicated over the batch or not.

I can see doing that with an OSFS instance (using `readlink`), but how would you do that for remote filesystems (such as an FTP) where the symlinks are traversed server-side?

```
u = "tar:///user/tmp/TAR/example.tar/bar/foo"
_fs = fs.open_fs(u)
```
The problem with this example is that there is no way to know that you aren't attempting to open a tar file called `foo`.

FS URLs do have a syntax that allows you to specify an additional path with a `!` as a separator. Here's an example:

```
from fs import opener
u = "tar:///user/tmp/TAR/example.tar!/bar/foo"
tar_fs, tar_path = opener.open(u)
```

But that doesn't actually open the directory for you. You would have to call `tar_fs.opendir(tar_path)` to open the directory.

IMO the best way to set this in angr is with just a global setting in the project (i.e. project.engines.vex) that can be overridden per-lift. A while ago as part of the performance problems it was observed there was a severe overhead with large list like objects in Ganga 6.1.
In order to try and combat this I tried to use a ```queues``` based system for initializing many objects and constructing a huge list with them which used to be done many times throughout the assignment process.
Unfortunately testing later showed that this code offered no performance improvement and list construction no longer appears to be the bottleneck.

However, the code remained.
As Ulrik pointed out this creates a bug that the assembled final list is not an ordered list so instead of trying to add complex logic to fix the multi-threaded case (which offered no performance improvement after-all) I've opted to drop to the single threaded case and remove the multi-threaded code which would now be inaccessible or commented out.

This PR also addresses the fact that there was object manipulation going on in the Proxy setter which was incorrect and was at least slowing down the assignment process and at worst potentially corrupting objects which were being externally passed to the setter as the new value.

aka, in some situations the following could potentially fail:
```
myObject = GangaObjectClass()
myObject2 = GangaObjectClass()
myObject.someAttribute = myObject2.someAttribute
assert isProxy(myObject2.someAttribute) != False
```
The following could lead to failure as the input value to the assignment ```myObject2.someAttribute``` was accidentally redefined as the proxy-stripped version of itself within the setter.

The policy that we decided on a long time ago was that a `GangaException` should not show a stack trace but that others should .The idea behind this is that a `GangaException` is something that the user can expect to get back to them whereas any other exception in principle indicates that we have a problem with the code (and that the stack trace then can help with the bug report). Are we either not implementing this idea, or is there a wish to change what we do?

It would be technically possible to store the n-1 set of traces, compare them and only print those which haven't changed. However, any monitoring threads or queues which are just waiting for work will also fit this criterion so it wouldn't necessarily provide the most useful feedback.

As far as I know, there isn't anything that's exactly like what you say -- there's only one circumstance where there's a special permission, and that's the `kinto.bucket_create_principals`, which lets you define some user that has permission to create buckets, even if they aren't granted any other permission in the database itself. If you had a fixed set of buckets, you might be able to set something up where you create all the buckets yourself and explicitly list the "superuser" among the permissions of that bucket. Because permissions "cascade", this would also grant them permissions on buckets and records. But you specifically said "without being explicitly listed in the permissions lists", and I don't think there's anything like that.

You might be able to implement something as a Kinto plugin which acts as a permission backend by just calling to some other permission backend and adds a "superuser" ID on every `get_object_permissions`. This wouldn't be a complete solution, but it might be good enough depending on your use case.

If you are able to build the kernel from source and your main goal is to get depth streaming (and not some of more advanced controls / metadata support), the core patches are relatively easy to apply by hand. 
You will need to add couple lines of code to `/drivers/media/usb/uvc/uvc_driver.c`, `/drivers/media/usb/uvc/uvcvideo.h` and `/include/uapi/linux/videodev2.h` to make V4L2 recognise `INZI`. You can use [the patch for Ubuntu](https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/blob/development/scripts/realsense-camera-formats_ubuntu-xenial-hwe-zesty.patch) as reference. 
Once you make the changes and successfully rebuild the kernel, it would be relatively easy to generate new `.patch` that you are welcomed to share here for future use. 
Alternatively, you can build the library with cmake flag `-DFORCE_LIBUVC=true` to circumvent the V4L2 sub-system. It will most likely work, but the result is less stable (hence we don't recommend it if you can patch uvcvideo instead)

4. The ``from_prev`` Firetasks *should* be modified to allow writing only the VASP input files they need to in order to operate. For example, the ``WriteVaspNSCFFromPrev`` would have an option to avoid rewriting the POTCAR. This is so if an upstream Firework made some change to the POTCAR settings, it would still be respected by the current FW rather than overwritten by the input set (when you really just wanted the input set to change a few INCAR settings). This might be a little problematic since sometimes these are coupled, e..g. removing or reordering sites from a POSCAR or switching functioanls in INCAR does necessitate rewriting the POTCAR. But it might be nice to have a ``skip_potcar`` option for some of these. I know I've sometimes changed a POTCAR upstream intentionally only to have it get re-changed back.

to my understanding, the problem is that re-running ppo2.learn with the same tensorflow graph tries to re-create trainable variables (including the state of the adam optimizer). While I agree that is a problem, I think we need a more principled solution - this problem will happen not only in ppo2; in most of the algorithms we use the same `trainer.compute_gradients` outside of explicit variable scope pattern. We'll either need to change all of them, or come up with some clever solution (maybe create scope within MpiAdamOptimizer).

Because the first can be supplied by a third party and hence easily spoofed.

Maybe "correct" isn't the right word, because this schema was designed specifically to have a certain degree of nebulosity. For example referential integrity is not guaranteed. You do not need to verify the entire hierarchy of data is correct for each and every query.

For example, say we have a `/tag/:tag_id` endpoint. If it's garbage data marked for deletion, it's probably fine to show, since that endpoint only assumes a certain view of the DB.

So I suspect the right thing to do, is only be as correct as particular situation requires, and no more.

Let me specify what my application needs. Maybe that will help clarify the optimal api.

We have a field ``p(x,y)`` on the interval ``[(0,Lx), (0,Ly)]``which needs to be Fourier transformed in x and y. Currently we use DFT, which implies that p is doubly periodic.

We would like to support "sidewalls" at either / both x=0,L and y=0,L, on which p = 0. The four cases would then be:

1. doubly periodic: use r2c DFT
2. p(0,y) = 0: use r2r DCT in x direction, r2c DFT in y direction
3. p(y,0) = 0: use r2r DCT in y direction, r2c DFT in x direction
4. p(0,y) = 0 AND p(x,0) = 0: use r2r DCT in both directions

If neither FFTW nor pyfftw supports 2 and 3 (e.g. via a tuple of transform types), then our application would have to extra logic to deal with those cases. Which would be fine of course but, from my perspective, preferable to deal with upstream. However, implementing this in pyfftw would break the simple correspondence with the FFTW api, so I understand that may not be desirable.

The problem is, we'd add a new dependency and break legacy code. Is this desirable for a marginal enhancement. What is the problem with the current cache? Why is the multithreading so bad?

The other problem is that we don't really tune the cache *size* so much as the number of cached items. It would be certainly be the case that having several big objects could overwhelm RAM and which would not be cleared easily.

@justinfx it is exactly thread **safe** as in "works without corrupting data", not thread optimal.

Two caveats:
- for new host (more precisely new conn_key) multiple threads may open multiple connections, one of them wins to store its connection to pool, it's not great but it is safe
- with your coming patch about removing timed out connection, a simple patch to pool reader is required to eliminate race, see below

Code, without irrelevant parts:

```python
            if conn_key in self.connections:
                conn = self.connections[conn_key]
            else:
                conn = self.connections[conn_key] = connection_type(..)
```

Suppose N threads read `conn_key not in dict` and create new connections and put it into dict. Exact connection put into pool is unknown. But all code below uses `conn` name which is separate for every thread, so we have potential connection burst, last write wins race, but no problems, it is still safe.

With your coming patch on removing timed out connections it is possible:
- thread 1 performs request, server is taking too long
- thread 2 reads `if conn in pool: True`
- thread 1 catches `socket.error` and removes conn from pool
- thread 2 proceeds into `conn = pool[key]` branch and has KeyError

Solution:

```diff
-            if conn_key in self.connections:
-                conn = self.connections[conn_key]
-            else:
+_MISSING = object()  # somewhere at top of module
+            conn = self.connections.get(conn_key, _MISSING)
+            if conn is _MISSING:
                 conn = self.connections[conn_key] = connection_type(..)
```

Hi @mgiaco,

cmd2 can easily do everything you need (plus a whole lot more).  The built-in **load** command runs commands one after another from a file.  It can be run directly from the interactive mode.  Alternatively, this can even be run directly from the command line.  Lets say your application which contains a class which inherits from cmd2.Cmd is in the file **project.py**, you can load and run a file (named "script.txt") of commands directly from the command line with:

```bash
python project.py "load script.txt"
```

By default, the interpreter will run every command in that file in sequence.  But if any command returns **True**, then that tells the interpreter that there was a problem and it exits early.

If you want a more complicated control flow, then the **pyscript** command enables running commands via a Python script and inspecting the results of previous commands as you go along, but that is a little more complicated.

I would recommend looking at and playing with some of the examples in the **examples** directory.

m.def("func1", [](const time_point<high_resolution_clock> &t) { ... });

On the other other hand, if `py::bases<...>` were used there would no need for the  `extract_all` metafunction.

> assuming the extra predicate doesn't affect compilation size

Predicates in the form of template aliases are extremely cheap (dare I say free). It's preferable to use an alias whenever possible: 
```c++
template<class T> using is_my_class = is_same<MyClass, T>; // alias
// vs.
template<class T> struct is_my_class : is_same<MyClass, T> {}; // new type
```
The alias is much lighter weight than the struct. The latter will instantiate a new type and the compiler needs to be aware of it (compiler memory and time). The alias is essentially just a replacement: it does not introduce a new type, thus it cannot be specialized and it does not appear as a unique type for overloads or class specializations.
Sounds good.

> Adding inline to member functions is either redundant or compiler dependent. They're usually handled with compatibility macros (STRONG_INLINE, ALWAYS_INLINE), if that's even needed here.

STRONG_INLINE and ALWAYS_INLINE are compiler-specific things. I generally dislike non-standard things. The `inline` keyword is the standard keyword showing intent of inlining. It is generally good practice for header-only library to specify non-template methods as inline.

@joohoi, do you know why this might be happening?

I've run into the same problem, it is caused by attempting to create a Python 2.7 virtual environment using a version of vitualenv from the Python 2.6 era. This is mostly caused by the logic on [line 152 of certbot-auto](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/ffc1874854fde7f61870780ce8684af2978b2434/certbot-auto#L132), which picks which python to pass to the `virtualenv` command's `--python` parameter:

    ...
    for LE_PYTHON in "$LE_PYTHON" python2.7 python27 python2 python; do
    ...

In an old distro such as Ubuntu Lucid, where the system Python is at 2.6 and virtualenv is at 1.4.5, with Python 2.7 installed alongside via the `python27` package (or from source via `make altinstall` in my case), this will result in invoking virtualenv 1.4.5 with `--python python2.7`, which will reproducibly throw the above "no module named warnings" error.

Not sure if this is a specific (known?) bug for virtualenv 1.4.5, and why that particular error is thrown, but that's what it is.

Not sure of the best solution here: changing the order on the line quoted above to prefer `python` over `python2.7` would fix it on old distros, but would break on newer distros where system Python is 3.x. 

Another option would be to perform a similar lookup for the best `virtualenv` command to use, preferring `virtualenv-2.7` or such, but that depends on such things existing and being installed, and in the case of Ubuntu Lucid, there doesn't appear to be `python27-virtualenv` package.

On the user end, this can be solved by either running certbot-auto with `env LE_PYTHON=python` (which would create the letsencrypt virtualenv with Python 2.6) or upgrading the system virtualenv (which would create the virtualenv with 2.7), so users can simply be told to do one of those in the documentation somewhere.

In my particular case, the former option resulted in segfaults thrown by the cryptography-python2.6 combo, but the latter worked just fine.

My understanding is that [dynamic DNS updates](https://ftp.isc.org/www/bind/arm95/Bv9ARM.ch04.html#dynamic_update) are not supposed to be immediately flushed to the master zone file after every update; you can use `rndc sync -clean` to force that flush, but that shouldn't have any affect on how your zones are resolving.

We also rely on [dnspython](https://github.com/rthalley/dnspython) to perform `nsupdate`s in this plugin, so this could be an upstream problem, or it could be an issue with your DNS setup.

If you need help debugging your DNS setup in general, I'd recommend posting on the [LetsEncrypt community forums](https://community.letsencrypt.org/c/help), where there's a larger audience of people who are also familiar with the project! You might get faster help there :D

TPOT pipeline parallelization is primarily done [here](https://github.com/EpistasisLab/tpot/blob/12eba6779793f77ae853476da8777397d22df502/tpot/base.py#L1168:L1178), where we're currently using scikit-learn's port of joblib.

Some additional considerations:

* **The parallelized evaluations need to be interruptible**, both because TPOT has options to stop evaluation on a per-pipeline basis (e.g., each pipeline gets 5 minutes to evaluate) and options to stop the optimization process after a fixed amount of time (e.g., TPOT gets 1 hour to evaluate as many pipelines as possible). Preferably the evaluations can be self-interruptible.

* **The parallelization procedure needs to be optional**, currently achieved with the `if` statement checking whether `n_jobs==1`.

A problem I see is that `state` is a reserved parameter for OAuth, it's purpose is to serve as a CSRF verification token to ensure that the request was generated from the same server, if you have your own `state` parameter, it will be replaced with the one needed to ensure the service security.

Another problem, is that not all the providers will accept extra parameters in the redirect URLs, or they might accept them, but not return them, so I wouldn't rely on this to get custom parameters back.

There's only one possible issue from the perspective of using the `Cognito` object.

Example:
Say I'm using this in a Django app.
A user inputs their credentials into a login form.
That input is used to instantiate a `Cognito` object, and then the `authenticate()` method is used.
If the `NEW_PASSWORD_REQUIRED` challenge comes up, then this call raises a key error when `Cognito` tries to pull tokens from the response.

Two ways of handling this could be either to catch the exception, and then call the `new_password_challenge method`. Or call the `admin_get_user` boto3 client method and check for a`'FORCE_CHANGE_PASSWORD` status on the user, so you would know in advance to use the `new_password_challenge` method.

With that said, I'm ok with getting this initial functionality in.

The [system_pkgs](https://github.com/ParallelSSH/ssh2-python/tree/system_pkgs) branch fixes the two issues above and results in RPMs that work correctly:

```
$ docker run -v "$(pwd):/src/" centos7 fpm --rpm-dist el7 -s python -t rpm -d libssh2 -d python setup.py
[root@6c8c0b197e8a src]# yum install python-ssh2-python-0.13.0.post2+3.g603a468.dirty-1.el7.x86_64.rpm -y
[root@6c8c0b197e8a src] cd
[root@6c8c0b197e8a ~]# python -c 'import ssh2.session'
[root@6c8c0b197e8a ~]# 
```

There are still a couple things needed before it can be merged and also similar changes needed to parallel-ssh as it has the same issue - see [122](https://github.com/ParallelSSH/parallel-ssh/issues/122). Will also re-enable system package builds on the releases page as well.

Note also that [parallel-ssh requires libssh2 >= 1.6](http://parallel-ssh.readthedocs.io/en/latest/installation.html#dependencies) if you intend to use that. The default package in centos7 is 1.4.2 from way back in 2012 and is not compatible. Any pre-built package >= 1.6 will work, or build from source with `./ci/build-ssh2.sh` in this repository.

Anything else not working as expected please raise an issue and will get looked at. Thanks for the interest.

In the meantime, after reviewing the log output, it's possible to run:
```

```
Which will update the product in the database. After we fix the bug, you would need to run again with the same option to convert back to the old definition.

I think the simplest approach is probably to have some sort of
save_exceptions flag you have to enable, since fail_after knows well
beforehand that it will want the exceptions, it can hide the extra
fiddliness from the user, and I don't have any other use cases in mind.
Capturing by default is tricky because there are sensible use cases where
cancel scope objects are saved and may inadvertantly outlive the stack
frame they apply to. (Like when you save off a cancel scope somewhere so
another task can find it and call its cancel method.)

Definetely I should make a more general statement that the order you run methods is not invariant for the _whole velocyto_. 

velocyto methods are meant to be run in particular steps of the analysis, so there is an implicit order and variations on this order does not guarantee you get correct results. This is a strong limitation, but, after all, velocyto.py does not aim to be a general purpose analysis tool like Seurat or PAGODA, its scope is profoundly different: it is supposed to do one thing as good as possible. 

For custom analyses, I assume that, users know exactly what they are doing (after all, velocyto runs on just simple and plain numpy under the hood): and throwing the error is right behaviour to me in this context (as an attention flag).

On mintty:

xterm```

On rxvt-native:
```$ echo $TERM
rxvt-cygwin-native```

From screen:
```$ echo $TERM
screen```
